destinations.tagline: Quiet luxury, powder-sand beaches, and a genuinely easy pace
destinations.vibe: Anguilla feels calm, refined, and less performative than some neighboring luxury islands. For Black travelers, it is a strong pick when you want a majority-Black Caribbean destination that still delivers high-end dining and beach time.
destinations.safetyNotes: Very comfortable for most travelers, with the main concerns being driving, beach isolation, and keeping track of belongings during island-hopping or boat days. Nightlife is mellow, so standard travel awareness usually covers it.

neighborhoods.name: The Valley
neighborhoods.description: Small capital area with practical services and local businesses.

neighborhoods.name: Shoal Bay area
neighborhoods.description: Beach-focused zone near one of the island’s signature stretches.

neighborhoods.name: West End
neighborhoods.description: Upscale area with villas, resorts, and sunset-facing beaches.

mustVisit.name: Shoal Bay East

mustVisit.name: Meads Bay
mustVisit.description: West-end beach lined with some of the island’s best-known stays and dining.

mustVisit.name: Sandy Ground
mustVisit.description: Harbor village with bars, boating, and a more casual social scene.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays
destinations.ratingNotes: Black-majority; quiet luxury; deep Afro-Caribbean roots

destinations.tagline: Temple-lined Nile days and slower evenings in Egypt’s open-air museum.
destinations.vibe: Luxor is more focused and easier to absorb than Cairo, with ancient sites shaping the whole city experience. For Black travelers, it often feels more tourism-driven than socially exploratory, so comfort depends a lot on your hotel base and tour setup.
destinations.safetyNotes: The main issues are heat, aggressive sales tactics, and transport negotiation more than random crime. Use licensed guides or hotel-arranged drivers for temple circuits and stay mindful of scams around major sites.

neighborhoods.name: East Bank
neighborhoods.description: Main hotel and restaurant side with easy Nile access.

neighborhoods.name: West Bank
neighborhoods.description: Quieter area near tombs, villages, and smaller guesthouses.

neighborhoods.name: Corniche
neighborhoods.description: Riverfront strip for hotels, walks, and evening views.

mustVisit.name: Karnak Temple Complex
mustVisit.description: Massive temple site and one of Luxor’s essential experiences.

mustVisit.name: Valley of the Kings
mustVisit.description: Royal burial site carved into desert hills on the West Bank.

mustVisit.name: Luxor Temple
mustVisit.description: Iconic temple glowing beautifully at night in the city center.

destinations.visaInfo: eVisa or visa required for many U.S. travelers to Egypt; verify current entry rules before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: Visible Nubian community but colorism present; tourist areas manageable

destinations.tagline: Black heritage, steakhouse city energy, and one of the Midwest's strongest hidden culture scenes
destinations.vibe: Omaha has more Black history and cultural substance than people give it credit for. For Black travelers, North Omaha heritage, jazz history, and a growing set of Black-created businesses make the city feel grounded, not generic.
destinations.safetyNotes: Omaha is manageable if you stay intentional about neighborhoods and transportation. The Old Market is the easiest tourist zone, but North Omaha is culturally important and worth visiting with a plan rather than through random exploration.

neighborhoods.name: Old Market
neighborhoods.description: Historic warehouse district with nightlife, dining, and easy first-time visitor appeal.

neighborhoods.name: North Omaha
neighborhoods.description: Center of Black history and community life in Omaha.

neighborhoods.name: Blackstone District
neighborhoods.description: Fast-growing food and social corridor with a modern urban feel.

mustVisit.name: Great Plains Black History Museum
mustVisit.description: Essential stop for understanding Omaha's African American heritage in regional context.

mustVisit.name: The Durham Museum
mustVisit.description: Beautiful station-turned-museum that gives depth to the city's broader history.

mustVisit.name: Joslyn Art Museum
mustVisit.description: Major art stop that adds balance to a heritage-focused itinerary.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Midwest; Malcolm X birthplace; limited Black infrastructure

destinations.tagline: A capital in motion with food, viewpoints, and comeback energy
destinations.vibe: San Salvador feels more like a fast-changing urban base than a traditional vacation fantasy, but it can surprise you with museums, food, and a strong sense of movement. For Black travelers, it makes the most sense as a curated city break where you lean on local recommendations and structured plans rather than improvising everything on the fly.
destinations.safetyNotes: San Salvador has improved its visitor experience, but it still calls for disciplined travel habits. Stick to established districts, use private transport rather than casual public transit, avoid intercity travel at night, and keep your plans tight after dark instead of wandering unfamiliar areas.

neighborhoods.name: Zona Rosa / San Benito
neighborhoods.description: Polished district with hotels, embassies, museums, and restaurants.

neighborhoods.name: Historic Center
neighborhoods.description: Restored core with landmarks, plazas, and civic architecture.

neighborhoods.name: Escalon
neighborhoods.description: Large district with dining, shopping, and residential calm.

mustVisit.name: National Palace
mustVisit.description: Landmark building anchoring the city’s historic center revival.

mustVisit.name: MARTE Museum
mustVisit.description: Top art museum with a stronger cultural feel than many visitors expect.

mustVisit.name: El Boqueron National Park
mustVisit.description: Easy nature escape with volcanic views just outside the city.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen for short tourist stays; tourist card may apply
destinations.ratingNotes: Central American capital; limited Black infrastructure; some discrimination

destinations.tagline: Mountain air, dark skies, and a different side of Arizona
destinations.vibe: Flagstaff feels refreshing if you want pine forests, Route 66 nostalgia, and a cooler, more outdoorsy Arizona base. For a Black traveler, it is not a diaspora-heavy destination, but it works well as a scenic reset with good walkability downtown and easy access to Northern Arizona landmarks.
destinations.safetyNotes: Flagstaff is one of the easier cities in this batch for visitors, especially downtown and around major trailheads and hotels. Standard mountain-town precautions apply: drive carefully in winter weather, do not leave valuables visible in parked cars, and prepare for thinner crowds outside the core at night.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown / Historic Route 66
neighborhoods.description: Walkable center with breweries, shops, hotels, and easy rail-town character.

neighborhoods.name: Southside
neighborhoods.description: Historic district near NAU with murals, diners, and local hangouts.

neighborhoods.name: University Heights / NAU Area
neighborhoods.description: Campus-adjacent zone with student energy and practical lodging options.

mustVisit.name: Lowell Observatory
mustVisit.description: Flagstaff classic for astronomy, night-sky programming, and city views.

mustVisit.name: Walnut Canyon National Monument
mustVisit.description: Dramatic cliff dwelling site just outside town.

mustVisit.name: Historic Downtown Flagstaff
mustVisit.description: The best compact area for restaurants, breweries, and a Route 66 feel.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Mountain college town; predominantly white; limited BOP

destinations.tagline: Big-island Greece with beaches, food, and ancient roots.
destinations.vibe: Crete gives you more breathing room than the party islands, with enough range to feel cultural, coastal, and restorative in one trip. For Black travelers, it is often easier to enjoy because you can choose calmer towns and avoid some of the scene-heavy pressure of smaller islands.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally very comfortable for visitors, with driving, rural roads, and summer crowds more important concerns than crime. As with much of Greece, some visitors may encounter curiosity in less-touristed areas, but serious incidents are uncommon.

neighborhoods.name: Chania Old Town
neighborhoods.description: Historic harbor quarter full of restaurants and waterfront charm.

neighborhoods.name: Heraklion Center
neighborhoods.description: Urban base near museums, shopping, and major transport links.

neighborhoods.name: Rethymno Old Town
neighborhoods.description: Walkable old center mixing Venetian history and beach access.

mustVisit.name: Knossos Palace
mustVisit.description: Major archaeological site tied to Minoan history.

mustVisit.name: Elafonissi Beach
mustVisit.description: Celebrated beach known for clear water and striking sand.

mustVisit.name: Heraklion Archaeological Museum
mustVisit.description: Key museum for understanding ancient Crete.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays in Greece/Schengen.
destinations.ratingNotes: Greek islands; some racism reported; limited Black community

destinations.tagline: A creative river town with mellow Midwest charm
destinations.vibe: Eau Claire feels artsy, outdoorsy, and a little more design-conscious than people expect. For Black travelers, it is less about diaspora infrastructure and more about whether you want a calm, scenic, small-city getaway with music, murals, and water nearby.
destinations.safetyNotes: Eau Claire is generally comfortable and straightforward for visitors. The main challenge is not safety so much as scale—this is a quieter city where you want to plan your evenings because things wind down earlier than in bigger markets.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Eau Claire
neighborhoods.description: Main dining, shopping, and culture district along the river.

neighborhoods.name: The Confluence Area
neighborhoods.description: Modern cultural zone anchored by arts venues and riverfront access.

neighborhoods.name: Carson Park Area
neighborhoods.description: Green, recreational district that feels family-friendly and spacious.

mustVisit.name: Carson Park
mustVisit.description: Classic park stop with trails, open space, and local recreation energy.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Wisconsin river town; very limited Black community

destinations.tagline: Easy island days with beaches, heritage villages, and a gentler Tunisia pace.
destinations.vibe: Djerba feels slower and softer than Tunis, with resort ease mixed with strong local identity. For Black travelers, it can feel more comfortable than larger capitals if you want a beach-forward trip, though island calm should not replace normal travel awareness.
destinations.safetyNotes: Resort zones are usually straightforward, with the main concerns being transport reliability, petty theft, and staying updated on broader national security guidance. Choose reputable accommodations and avoid poorly lit stretches late at night outside main tourist corridors.

neighborhoods.name: Midoun

mustVisit.name: Plage de Sidi Mahrez
mustVisit.description: Well-known beach strip for easy sand-and-sea downtime.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays in Tunisia; verify current entry rules before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: North African island; Afro-Tunisian community present; generally welcoming

destinations.tagline: Sky-high capital with grit, cable cars, and deep culture
destinations.vibe: La Paz feels dramatic, layered, and less polished than postcard South America, which is part of its pull. For Black travelers, it works best as a culture-forward city where you lean into markets, viewpoints, and the Indigenous backbone of the place.
destinations.safetyNotes: Altitude, traffic, and petty theft are the main issues for most visitors. Stay alert in crowded markets and transit areas, avoid flashing valuables, and use radio taxis or ride apps at night; some travelers also report staring because Black visitors are still relatively uncommon.

neighborhoods.name: Zona Sur

mustVisit.name: Witches' Market

mustVisit.name: Valle de la Luna

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays
destinations.ratingNotes: Indigenous Andean capital; anti-Black discrimination documented

destinations.tagline: Nigeria’s polished capital with space, style, and big-weekend energy.
destinations.vibe: Abuja feels more planned, spread out, and visibly affluent than Lagos, with wide roads, lounges, and a diplomatic-city finish. For Black travelers, it can feel comfortable and familiar culturally, but it still rewards structure, local contacts, and solid logistics.
destinations.safetyNotes: Upscale districts and known hospitality venues are usually more manageable, but crime, road risks, and security volatility remain real concerns. Use vetted drivers, avoid unnecessary late-night movement, and stay current on local security conditions before exploring widely.

neighborhoods.name: Wuse 2

destinations.ratingNotes: Black-majority Nigerian capital; no racial discrimination against Black visitors

destinations.tagline: Afro-Caribbean island life with surf, color, and zero rush
destinations.vibe: Bocas del Toro feels loose, sun-soaked, and openly Caribbean, the kind of place where boat rides, beach bars, and slow mornings set the tone. For Black travelers, the Afro-Caribbean cultural presence gives it a familiarity and warmth that can make it feel different from more generic island escapes.
destinations.safetyNotes: Bocas is usually easier than a major city, but it still comes with island-specific caution points. Watch your belongings around docks and nightlife, be smart with water activities and boat logistics, and do not assume late-night party energy means every area is equally safe or easy to navigate.

neighborhoods.name: Bocas Town
neighborhoods.description: Main hub on Isla Colon for hotels, nightlife, and boat connections.

neighborhoods.name: Bluff Beach Area

neighborhoods.name: Bastimentos

destinations.tagline: Jamaica holds a special place in the Black diaspora — birthplace of reggae and Rastafarianism
Route 66 energy, desert beauty, and a strong Black history thread
destinations.vibe: Albuquerque has real personality—less polished than Santa Fe, but broader, more lived-in, and easier to sink into. For Black travelers, the city stands out because New Mexico's Black history is deeper than many people realize, and Albuquerque gives you both culture and a practical city base.
destinations.safetyNotes: Albuquerque requires normal city caution plus some extra planning around nightlife and late-night movement. Old Town, Nob Hill, and museum areas are easier for visitors, while some central corridors can feel more unpredictable after dark.

neighborhoods.name: Old Town
neighborhoods.description: Historic visitor district with adobe architecture, shops, and museums.

neighborhoods.name: Nob Hill

mustVisit.name: Albuquerque Museum

destinations.tagline: Louisiana’s capital with real Black history and local flavor
destinations.vibe: Baton Rouge has weight to it: Southern University pride, civil rights history and a food scene that feels rooted rather than manufactured. From a Black traveler perspective, the city has cultural substance and everyday Black presence, even if it is less polished than New Orleans.
destinations.safetyNotes: Visitors do best staying alert downtown, around LSU-adjacent areas and in established dining zones. Baton Rouge has real cultural payoff, but it is also a place where neighborhood awareness matters more than in smaller college towns; driving between destinations is smarter than improvising on foot, especially at night.

destinations.tagline: Adventure-country ease where the Nile and creative local energy meet.
destinations.vibe: Jinja feels smaller, greener, and more outdoorsy than Kampala, with rafting culture and a more relaxed traveler rhythm. For Black travelers, it can feel refreshing because the focus is on experiences and scenery more than big-city posturing.
destinations.safetyNotes: Jinja is generally easier to navigate than Kampala, but river activities and road travel add their own risks. Book adventure operators carefully, keep nighttime transport simple, and do not let the resort-town mood make you careless with valuables.

destinations.tagline: A polished capital city with major Black history beneath the surface
destinations.vibe: Des Moines feels orderly, easy, and more cosmopolitan than outsiders expect. For Black travelers, the city's story gets stronger when you connect its polished present with the legacy of Center Street and Fort Des Moines.
destinations.safetyNotes: Des Moines is generally comfortable for visitors, with downtown, East Village, and West Des Moines areas feeling straightforward. As always, the experience improves when you know the context and seek out the city's Black heritage instead of only its generic capital-city highlights.

destinations.tagline: Colonial calm, Yucatecan flavor, and easy luxury
destinations.vibe: Merida feels elegant, slower, and more comfortable than many first-time Mexico travelers expect, with beautiful architecture and a strong food culture. For Black travelers, it reads as a city for relaxed evenings, day trips, and stylish stays rather than one built around nightlife or visible diaspora scenes.
destinations.safetyNotes: Merida is widely regarded as one of the easier Mexican cities for travelers to navigate, especially in and around the center and Paseo de Montejo. Normal awareness still matters, but the city’s biggest practical issues for many visitors are heat, midday pacing, and transport planning more than personal safety.

destinations.tagline: Music legacy, lakefront energy, and a city still rewriting its story
destinations.vibe: Gary hits differently if you approach it through Black cultural history instead of mainstream tourism. For Black travelers, the Jackson family legacy, industrial grit, and Lake Michigan access make it feel like a city with scars, pride, and real significance.
destinations.safetyNotes: Gary requires more planning than most cities in this batch. Stick to specific destinations, go in daylight, use a car, and avoid casual exploration in unfamiliar residential blocks after dark.

destinations.tagline: A moody strait-side city where Africa and Europe feel close enough to touch.
destinations.vibe: Tangier mixes port-city grit, literary legacy, and breezy sea views. For Black travelers, it can feel stylish and more relaxed than expected, especially in its café and riad scene, though the medina still rewards confidence and patience.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally manageable for visitors, with common concerns centered on pickpocketing, taxi confusion, and pressure from touts around transit points. Stick to well-reviewed lodging, keep valuables close in the medina, and use licensed taxis.

destinations.tagline: Orange blossoms, flamenco nights, and Andalusian heat.
destinations.vibe: Seville is warm, dramatic, and deeply social, with beautiful streets that invite slow walking and long dinners. Black travelers often find it welcoming on the surface, though the city is less diverse than Madrid or Barcelona and can feel more traditional.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually easy to navigate, with petty theft the main concern in tourist zones near the cathedral and major transit routes. Summer heat is intense, so daytime planning matters as much as personal safety.

destinations.tagline: Big-city chaos, late-night food, and temple grandeur that never really slows down.
destinations.vibe: Bangkok can feel thrilling, overwhelming, and deeply rewarding all in one day—street food, rooftop bars, temples, and neighborhoods with distinct personalities. For Black travelers, it usually works best when you move with confidence and ignore the occasional novelty attention that can come with being visibly foreign.
destinations.safetyNotes: Widely traveled and usually manageable, with the biggest issues being scams, nightlife judgment, and petty theft rather than overt danger. Use licensed transport, watch drink safety, and stay sharper in red-light or party-heavy zones.

destinations.tagline: A nonstop megacity where ancient wonders and everyday hustle collide.
destinations.vibe: Cairo is intense, iconic, and full of rhythm—one minute you are by the Nile, the next you are in a centuries-old market. For Black travelers, the city can feel visually overwhelming more than personally hostile, but firm boundaries and a trusted guide can make the experience smoother.
destinations.safetyNotes: Expect heavy traffic, assertive vendors, and occasional harassment in crowded tourist zones rather than constant danger everywhere. Stick to vetted drivers, avoid political gatherings, and be especially alert after dark outside major hotel and museum areas.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Cairo
neighborhoods.description: Historic core with belle-époque buildings, museums, and nonstop street life.

destinations.tagline: Royal-palace history and Dahomey legacy at the root of West African memory.
destinations.vibe: Abomey is less about nightlife or convenience and more about lineage, power, and history that matters. For Black travelers, it can feel deeply meaningful because the city sits so close to conversations about kingdoms, resistance, and the wider diaspora story.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually calmer than larger regional capitals, but infrastructure is lighter and visitor support is less polished. Daytime exploration is easiest, road travel should be planned carefully, and French-speaking help goes a long way.

destinations.ratingNotes: Royal Dahomey heritage; Black-majority Benin; diaspora roots here

destinations.tagline: An easygoing capital that works best as a polished Southern Africa base.
destinations.vibe: Gaborone is calm, practical, and less about spectacle than stability. For Black travelers, it often feels comfortable and straightforward, especially if you want a clean, low-drama entry point before safaris or longer regional travel.
destinations.safetyNotes: Botswana is generally considered one of the steadier countries in the region for visitors, though normal urban precautions still apply. Be cautious with isolated streets at night and keep transport arrangements simple outside core business and hotel areas.

mustVisit.name: Mokolodi Nature Reserve
mustVisit.description: Wildlife reserve close to the city for an easy nature day trip.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays in Botswana; verify current entry rules before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: Stable democracy; welcoming; very low discrimination risk

destinations.tagline: Mountain calm with a deeper Black story than people expect
destinations.vibe: Boulder can feel beautiful, healthy, and very white at first glance, but it also has meaningful Black history and a more layered cultural story than its branding suggests. For Black travelers, it is best enjoyed as a nature-meets-history stop rather than a full diaspora destination.
destinations.safetyNotes: Boulder is physically easy and generally comfortable for visitors, especially downtown and around Pearl Street. The bigger issue for some Black travelers may be social feel rather than personal safety, since the city can read as affluent and culturally homogenous in certain spaces.

mustVisit.name: Museum of Boulder

mustVisit.name: Pearl Street Mall
mustVisit.description: Classic Boulder pedestrian zone and the easiest first stop in town.

mustVisit.name: Chautauqua Park
mustVisit.description: Iconic Boulder nature access point with great views and easy trails.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Predominantly white; very limited Black community; progressive but homogeneous

destinations.tagline: Big-business Morocco with Atlantic views and modern city swagger.
destinations.vibe: Casablanca is less postcard-pretty and more about energy, design, and local momentum. For Black travelers, it often feels easier than some European cities in terms of blending into the wider social fabric, though street smarts still matter in busy transit and downtown zones.
destinations.safetyNotes: Petty theft and hustling are the main concerns in crowded areas, not constant personal threat. Stay alert around the medina, train stations, and late-night transit, and use reputable taxis or ride services after dark.

mustVisit.name: La Corniche
mustVisit.description: Atlantic promenade lined with cafés, hotels, and nightlife spots.

mustVisit.name: Rick’s Café
mustVisit.description: Stylized restaurant-bar inspired by the city’s cinematic mythology.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays in Morocco.
destinations.ratingNotes: Business capital; colorism documented; sub-Saharan travelers face challenges

destinations.tagline: A dense capital of malls, memory, and nonstop Filipino city energy.
destinations.vibe: Manila is not subtle—it is crowded, layered, and often chaotic, but it rewards travelers who understand big-city rhythm and know how to edit their itinerary. For Black travelers, it can feel welcoming socially while still bringing visible-foreigner attention and the usual capital-city friction.
destinations.safetyNotes: Traffic, scams, petty theft, and uneven neighborhood quality matter more than dramatic tourist danger in most cases. Keep your movements intentional, choose neighborhoods carefully, and use reputable transport rather than improvising at night.

neighborhoods.name: Makati
neighborhoods.description: Business and nightlife district with polished hotels, malls, and expat infrastructure.

neighborhoods.name: Bonifacio Global City

neighborhoods.name: Intramuros

mustVisit.name: Intramuros
mustVisit.description: Walled historic district that anchors much of Manila’s tourism story.

mustVisit.name: National Museum of Fine Arts
mustVisit.description: Strong museum stop for Philippine art and national identity.

mustVisit.name: Mall of Asia Bay Area
mustVisit.description: Popular waterfront retail-entertainment zone for sunset, shopping, and nightlife spillover.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for many short U.S. citizen tourist stays, subject to current Philippines entry rules; verify before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: Colorism significant; generally curious/neutral but discrimination present

destinations.tagline: Mediterranean design capital with beach energy and big-city style
destinations.vibe: Barcelona feels visual, social, and effortlessly photogenic, with architecture and neighborhood life doing as much work as the landmarks. From a Black traveler perspective, it is best when you enjoy the city with intention because the cosmopolitan vibe is real, but so are occasional racialized nightlife issues or microaggressions.
destinations.safetyNotes: A highly visitable city, but petty theft is one of the biggest practical risks, especially around transit, crowded avenues, and nightlife zones. Black travelers often report that the city is manageable overall, but it is worth choosing venues carefully and staying alert to both pickpockets and occasional bias in social spaces.

neighborhoods.name: Eixample
neighborhoods.description: Elegant grid district with Gaudí landmarks, shopping, and strong hotel options.

neighborhoods.name: El Born

neighborhoods.name: Barceloneta

mustVisit.name: La Pedrera / Casa Milà
mustVisit.description: Gaudí landmark that captures Barcelona’s modernist identity.

mustVisit.name: Picasso Museum
mustVisit.description: Major art stop inside the old city’s medieval fabric.

mustVisit.name: Barceloneta Beach
mustVisit.description: City beach where the Mediterranean side of Barcelona comes fully alive.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays up to 90 days in the Schengen Area
destinations.ratingNotes: Tourist hub; some discrimination; limited Black-owned ecosystem

destinations.tagline: Appalachian college-town energy with hidden Black history
destinations.vibe: Athens, Ohio, is funky, green and student-driven, with live music, old brick streets and a strong DIY spirit. For Black travelers, the city gets more meaningful when you dig into the Mount Zion story and the broader Black Appalachian history that many visitors would otherwise miss.
destinations.safetyNotes: Uptown and Ohio University-adjacent areas are generally the easiest for visitors, especially when school is in session and streets stay active. The bigger issue here is not high danger but limited diversity and the feeling of being conspicuous in some spaces, so move with confidence and keep late-night plans centered in busy areas.

neighborhoods.name: Ohio University Area

neighborhoods.name: West Side / Mount Zion Area

destinations.tagline: Most popular international destination for Black American travelers
destinations.ratingNotes: Resort corridor; anti-Black discrimination reported; few Black-owned

destinations.tagline: Tobacco-town legacy with a civil rights story to tell
destinations.vibe: Danville has a strong sense of place, with riverfront redevelopment and a civil-rights-era history that deserves more attention than it gets. For Black travelers, the city feels most meaningful when explored through its Black heritage tours and local history rather than as a generic weekend escape.
destinations.safetyNotes: The River District and main museum areas are the easiest parts of Danville to navigate. Like several small Southern cities, it is better experienced with a clear plan than through spontaneous wandering; the social climate can feel traditional, so heritage-led travel often produces the strongest experience for Black visitors.

neighborhoods.name: River District

destinations.ratingNotes: Small Virginia city; tobacco history; limited Black infrastructure

destinations.tagline: Benin’s coastal hustle with markets, art, and a gateway feel.
destinations.vibe: Cotonou feels energetic, humid, and commercially alive—a place you move through with intention rather than romance. For Black travelers, it can work well as an access point to Benin’s deeper cultural sites while still offering strong market life, music, and food.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally manageable with practical caution, though petty theft, traffic risk, and late-night judgment matter. Keep valuables tight in market areas, use trusted rides, and avoid treating the beach at night like a resort strip.

destinations.tagline: Medina magic, deep craft heritage, and one of Morocco’s richest old-city experiences.
destinations.vibe: Fes feels immersive and historical, with the medina doing most of the storytelling. For Black travelers, it can feel culturally rich and visually affirming, but the old city can also be tiring if you are navigating touts and sensory overload without a guide.
destinations.safetyNotes: The main risks are getting lost, overpaying, and dealing with persistent unsolicited guides in the medina. Stay in well-reviewed riads, arrange transfers ahead, and avoid isolated alleys late at night.

destinations.tagline: Grit, grand boulevards, and a nightlife scene that surprises people.
destinations.vibe: Bucharest is less polished than Europe’s headline cities, but that is part of the appeal if you like destinations with texture and contrast. For Black travelers, it can feel curious rather than diverse, so confidence and good neighborhood choices make a difference.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually navigable, but some areas can feel rougher late at night and transit awareness matters. Petty theft and nightlife judgment calls are bigger concerns than violent crime for most visitors.

destinations.tagline: Eternal-spring weather with style, movement, and reinvention
destinations.vibe: Medellín has charisma: good weather, a visible sense of reinvention, and enough neighborhoods with strong personalities to make every day feel different. For Black travelers, it can be a fun, social trip if you balance nightlife with culture and stay conscious that the city’s polished image does not erase the need for street smarts.
destinations.safetyNotes: Medellín is easy to romanticize, but visitors still need caution around nightlife, dating setups, and flashy displays of money or phones. Stick to known areas, use app-based transport, and keep your plans intentional after dark instead of assuming the city’s popularity means zero risk.

destinations.tagline: Borderland culture with beach access and understated depth
destinations.vibe: Brownsville feels bicultural, warm and local-first, with a slower pace than Texas cities farther north. For Black travelers, it is more about discovering a less obvious corner of the country than chasing a large Black social scene, so expectations should be culture-forward rather than diaspora-forward.
destinations.safetyNotes: The main visitor zones and daytime attractions are usually manageable, but as in many border cities, context matters block by block and late-night roaming is unnecessary. Black travelers may notice curiosity from being visibly outside the local norm more than direct hostility; stay in active areas and use a car for convenience and ease.

destinations.tagline: An underrated Iowa city with one of the state's strongest Black heritage anchors
destinations.vibe: Cedar Rapids is not flashy, but it has a useful mix of neighborhood calm, Midwestern affordability, and serious African American history infrastructure. For Black travelers, the museum scene gives the city more depth than its national profile suggests.
destinations.safetyNotes: Cedar Rapids is fairly manageable for visitors, especially in daytime and around NewBo, downtown, and museum corridors. Like many smaller cities, it is best enjoyed with a car and a plan rather than late-night wandering between districts.

destinations.tagline: Modern capital energy with more substance than spectacle.
destinations.vibe: Ankara is less romantic than Istanbul but more straightforward if you want museums, politics, and everyday Turkish city life. For Black travelers, it is usually a practical stop rather than a dreamy one, and the experience depends heavily on why you are there and where you stay.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally orderly in central areas, but the broader Türkiye risk picture and occasional demonstrations mean you should stay current on conditions. It is more businesslike than touristy, so choosing central, well-reviewed areas helps a lot.

destinations.tagline: The home of Caribbean Carnival — the original, most elaborate in the Western Hemisphere
Sacred history, layered identity, and intense atmosphere.
destinations.vibe: Jerusalem is profound and emotionally charged, the kind of place where faith, politics, and daily life are impossible to separate. For Black travelers, the city can be unforgettable, but it is not a casual add-on destination right now and should be approached with seriousness.
destinations.safetyNotes: Security conditions and political tension are the dominant concern, far more than ordinary tourism logistics. Travelers should monitor official advisories closely, avoid demonstrations and flashpoint areas, and be prepared for plans to change quickly.

destinations.tagline: Brainy, leafy and richer in Black history than people expect
destinations.vibe: Ann Arbor is clean, walkable and intellectually charged, with plenty of bookshops, cafés and museum energy. From a Black traveler perspective, it works best when you tap into the city’s African American history and nearby Ypsi influence instead of treating it like just another college town.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown, Kerrytown and campus-adjacent areas are generally easy to navigate. Most Black travelers will likely experience Ann Arbor as calm but somewhat polished and less openly expressive than Detroit; the main issue is occasional social awkwardness or feeling watched in upscale spaces, rather than high personal risk.

destinations.tagline: Ultra-clean city-state ease with hawker food and global-city polish.
destinations.vibe: Singapore feels efficient, curated, and very easy to enjoy if you like cities that run well. For Black travelers, it often feels low-friction and comfortable, with multicultural neighborhoods and strong public order balancing out the city’s polished, tightly regulated energy.
destinations.safetyNotes: One of the easiest major cities in the world to move through safely, with low violent-crime risk by visitor standards. The real adjustment is strict rules and high prices, not personal security in the usual sense.

destinations.tagline: Pacific views and one of Latin America’s best food scenes
destinations.vibe: Lima is a city for people who travel by appetite, neighborhood, and atmosphere rather than postcard perfection. For Black travelers, it can be a strong mix of food, oceanfront calm, and culture if you base yourself in the right districts and treat the city as a layered capital instead of just a stop before Cusco.
destinations.safetyNotes: Lima is usually straightforward in districts like Miraflores, Barranco, and San Isidro, but you still want standard big-city caution. Use rideshare at night, keep valuables close in crowded areas, and know that the experience changes a lot block to block once you move outside the most visitor-friendly zones.

destinations.tagline: Imperial beauty with café culture and clean-city calm.
destinations.vibe: Vienna feels composed and beautiful, almost like the city is trying not to break a sweat. For Black travelers, it is often smooth and rewarding if you enjoy museums, music, and architecture, though the social atmosphere can feel reserved rather than warm.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually very safe by big-city standards, with petty theft in crowded transit and tourist spots the biggest issue. The main friction is more likely to be stiffness or occasional insensitive behavior than overt danger.

destinations.tagline: Color, craft, and one of the strongest food cities anywhere
destinations.vibe: Oaxaca feels intimate, artful, and rooted, the kind of place where meals, markets, and street life become the whole trip. For Black travelers, it is less about big-city nightlife and more about culture, texture, and slowing down enough to really connect with local craft, mezcal, and community rhythms.
destinations.safetyNotes: Oaxaca City is usually one of the easier Mexico destinations for visitors to enjoy on foot, especially around the center and major dining corridors. Petty theft and late-night overconfidence are the main things to watch for, so keep valuables close and use a taxi or rideshare back after evenings out.

destinations.tagline: Hilltop hustle, nightlife, and a gateway into Uganda’s broader beauty.
destinations.vibe: Kampala is energetic, social, and loud in a way that rewards flexibility more than rigid plans. For Black travelers, it often feels familiar in its people-centered warmth, though the city can also be exhausting if traffic, road conditions, and nightlife logistics are not planned well.
destinations.safetyNotes: The bigger issues are traffic chaos, petty crime, and security uncertainty rather than every neighborhood feeling unsafe all the time. Stay alert at night, avoid political gatherings, use trusted drivers, and choose lodging in established areas with good reviews.

neighborhoods.name: Kololo

destinations.tagline: Radical history, great food, and an easy Bay side quest
destinations.vibe: Berkeley works best if you like ideas, bookstores, and meals that turn into long conversations. For a Black traveler, it is less about a dominant Black cultural footprint than nearby Oakland, but it still offers activist history, campus energy, and a thoughtful, low-pressure Bay Area stay.
destinations.safetyNotes: Berkeley is generally comfortable for visitors, especially downtown, the Gourmet Ghetto, and campus-adjacent blocks. Standard urban awareness applies around transit and parking, but the city is one of the easier, more walkable stops in the batch.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Berkeley

neighborhoods.name: Southside

destinations.tagline: Sailor-chic island charm without the heavy gloss
destinations.vibe: Bequia feels friendly, sea-breezy, and intimate, with enough polish for a romantic escape but still plenty of local character. For Black travelers, the relaxed Caribbean social rhythm is part of its appeal.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually very comfortable and low-pressure, with hills, taxis, and ferry timing more annoying than personal safety. Still use standard beach and bar awareness, especially when returning to villas after dark.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays
destinations.ratingNotes: Authentic small-island; Black-majority; sailor charm

destinations.tagline: Mariachi pride with big-city flavor and old-school swagger
destinations.vibe: Guadalajara feels confident, cultured, and better paced than Mexico City, with enough nightlife and design energy to keep a trip lively. For Black travelers, it works best when you mix the polished neighborhoods, tequila-country day trips, and local music culture instead of treating it like a one-note party stop.
destinations.safetyNotes: Guadalajara is manageable for visitors, especially in neighborhoods like Americana and Zapopan-adjacent districts, but nightlife areas call for real awareness. Use rideshare at night, avoid flashing valuables, and keep your plans centered in areas with steady foot traffic rather than wandering unfamiliar zones late.

destinations.tagline: River days, outlet runs, and a laid-back college-town rhythm
destinations.vibe: San Marcos is best if you want an easy Central Texas weekend built around the river, downtown bars, and a younger crowd. For a Black traveler, it reads more as a relaxed tubing-and-food trip than a culture-heavy destination, but it is friendly, affordable, and well placed between Austin and San Antonio.
destinations.safetyNotes: San Marcos is generally easy for visitors, especially around the square, Texas State, and river recreation zones in daytime. During busy weekends and tubing season, stay alert around crowded parks and parking areas, and rely on rideshare rather than wandering far late at night.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Square

destinations.tagline: River-city grit with meaningful Black history stops
destinations.vibe: Huntington has a blue-collar, creative-understory feel that becomes much more compelling once you connect it to Carter G. Woodson and the city’s Black institutions. For Black travelers, it is strongest as a thoughtful heritage-and-arts stop rather than a flashy getaway.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown Huntington and Marshall-adjacent areas are the easiest places to spend time, while some quieter stretches can feel less comfortable late at night. The city can feel welcoming in pockets, especially when your itinerary is centered on cultural institutions, but it is still best approached with normal mid-sized-city caution and a car.

destinations.tagline: Farm-to-fork energy with more soul than people expect
destinations.vibe: Sacramento often surprises people because it mixes government-town structure with murals, great food, and a much warmer local vibe than its reputation suggests. For a Black traveler, it is a good city for a low-friction weekend built around food, arts districts, and local pace rather than nonstop spectacle.
destinations.safetyNotes: Sacramento is generally straightforward for visitors in Midtown, Downtown Commons, and riverfront areas, though the usual city precautions still matter late at night. Use extra care with parking and choose well-reviewed nightlife zones instead of wandering into quiet stretches after hours.

destinations.tagline: A practical Southern Africa hub with markets, museums, and onward-adventure appeal.
destinations.vibe: Lusaka is more functional than flashy, but that makes it useful for travelers who care about access and authenticity. For Black travelers, it often feels socially comfortable, though the city experience is strongest when paired with day trips or a wider Zambia itinerary.
destinations.safetyNotes: Urban petty crime and road safety are bigger concerns than destination-wide instability. Use reputable taxis or drivers, avoid carrying too much cash, and stay cautious around bus stations and isolated streets after dark.

destinations.tagline: Small-town Virginia with local Black history worth seeking out
destinations.vibe: Martinsville is quieter and less destination-driven than most cities here, but it has a meaningful Black community history and a revitalizing downtown feel. For Black travelers, this works better as a regional stop where you move intentionally between heritage sites and local institutions.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown and official museum stops are the best anchors for a visit. Because the city is smaller and less tourism-centered, comfort depends a lot on planning your route and expectations; Black travelers are likely to encounter a more old-school social atmosphere than in bigger university or creative hubs.

destinations.tagline: Country-soul roots and a practical California stopover
destinations.vibe: Bakersfield has more personality than outsiders give it credit for, especially once you tap into the music legacy and local food culture. From a Black travel perspective, this is more of a grounded, road-trip-friendly city than a polished tourism destination, but it can still be worth a stop for history and live music texture.
destinations.safetyNotes: Bakersfield is best handled with the same awareness you'd use in any spread-out car city: choose your districts carefully and keep nights intentional. Stick to downtown, the arts-adjacent corridors, and established venues, and don't treat the city like a late-night walking destination.

destinations.tagline: Whitewashed luxury with beach clubs and island glow.
destinations.vibe: Mykonos is all about atmosphere, image, and high-energy summer travel, so it works best if you are leaning into that. For Black travelers, it can feel glamorous and freeing, especially in LGBTQ+ and nightlife spaces, but it is also one of the batch’s most expensive and performative destinations.
destinations.safetyNotes: Personal safety is generally solid, but nightlife, alcohol, and high-spend environments create their own risk. Plan transport in advance, watch your valuables in clubs and on beaches, and expect premium pricing everywhere in season.

destinations.tagline: High-altitude history with sacred-valley energy
destinations.vibe: Cusco feels textured, spiritual, and tourism-heavy in the best and worst ways. From a Black traveler perspective, it shines when you slow down for the plazas, markets, and Inca-meets-colonial layers instead of treating it like only a Machu Picchu stop.
destinations.safetyNotes: The historic center is usually manageable with normal travel awareness, but tourist scams, pickpocketing, and aggressive sales pitches can wear on you. Black travelers may get extra curiosity more than overt hostility, so use confident boundaries, airport transfers, and trusted taxis.

destinations.tagline: Ashanti heritage, market power, and the cultural heart of inland Ghana.
destinations.vibe: Kumasi is less beachy and internationally polished than Accra, but culturally it hits harder if you want heritage, craft, and Ashanti history. For Black travelers, it can feel especially rich because the city’s identity is so clearly tied to its own traditions and institutions.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually manageable for travelers who plan their transport and respect the pace of a busy regional capital. Markets can be overwhelming, traffic can be chaotic, and nighttime wandering is less appealing than guided or destination-focused movement.

destinations.tagline: Beach city ease with real neighborhood culture
destinations.vibe: Long Beach gives you coastal California without forcing the full LA performance. For a Black traveler, it can be a great fit because the city feels diverse, creative, and easier to navigate than many nearby Southern California options, with good food, waterfront views, and neighborhoods that each have their own personality.
destinations.safetyNotes: Long Beach is generally good for visitors, especially in Belmont Shore, Downtown, and waterfront zones, though parking and car break-ins remain worth taking seriously. Stay aware after dark in less trafficked blocks and use standard rideshare judgment when moving between nightlife districts.

destinations.tagline: Fashion, diamonds, and port-city style.

destinations.safetyNotes: Generally comfortable, with normal caution needed around the central station, nightlife zones, and crowded shopping areas. Direct hostility is uncommon, but like much of Europe, subtle bias can show up more than overt incidents.

destinations.tagline: Wine-country calm at the foot of the Andes
destinations.vibe: Mendoza feels slower, sunnier, and more spacious than Buenos Aires, with a trip rhythm built around wine, long meals, and mountain day trips. For Black travelers, it reads as a scenic lifestyle destination more than a diaspora destination, but the quality-of-life vibe is the draw.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally comfortable for visitors around the main squares and winery circuits, though quiet streets can empty out late. Use normal city caution after dark and avoid leaving phones or bags visible in cars on winery outings.

destinations.tagline: Atlantic Angola with big-city ambition, music, and sharp contrasts.

destinations.safetyNotes: Crime is a serious practical concern in the Luanda metro area, and logistics can get expensive fast. Stay in secure accommodations, use vetted drivers, avoid flashing valuables, and keep nighttime movement tightly controlled.

destinations.tagline: A practical capital with museums, coffee, and easy launch-point energy

destinations.safetyNotes: San Jose is manageable in the right areas, but like many regional capitals it rewards planning over improvisation. Stick to central districts with visible activity, be cautious with phones and bags downtown, and use registered taxis or rideshare after dark rather than walking long distances.

destinations.safetyNotes: Petty crime, cash issues, and infrastructure inconsistencies are more relevant than constant violent threat. Use trusted transport, carry backup payment options, and keep plans flexible in case services or exchange conditions shift.

destinations.tagline: Authentic small-island Caribbean with real local tempo
destinations.vibe: Carriacou feels intimate, unhurried, and refreshingly less manufactured than bigger Caribbean names. From a Black traveler perspective, it is more about community, water, and genuine island rhythm than headline luxury.
destinations.safetyNotes: Very mellow overall, though transport options are limited and some beaches or roads can feel isolated. Keep cash, ferry plans, and night driving organized, and do not expect big-island infrastructure.

neighborhoods.name: Hillsborough
neighborhoods.description: Main town and ferry hub with shops, guesthouses, and everyday island life.

neighborhoods.name: Tyrell Bay
neighborhoods.description: Protected bay area popular with sailors and waterfront bars.

neighborhoods.name: Windward
neighborhoods.description: Traditional village area known for boatbuilding heritage.

mustVisit.name: Paradise Beach
mustVisit.description: Clear-water beach ideal for relaxed swimming and low-key lounging.

mustVisit.name: Tyrell Bay
mustVisit.description: Scenic harbor zone that captures the island’s sailing culture.

mustVisit.name: Belmont Estate / local heritage stops
mustVisit.description: Good way to connect Carriacou to broader Grenadian cultural context.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays
destinations.ratingNotes: Authentic Caribbean; Black-majority; real local tempo

destinations.tagline: Big-coast energy, Swahili culture, and the pulse before Zanzibar or safari.
destinations.vibe: Dar is humid, sprawling, and alive, with beaches, markets, and business-city momentum all at once. For Black travelers, it often feels culturally intuitive because the city is so deeply African and coastal, but pace and planning still matter.
destinations.safetyNotes: Petty theft, transport confusion, and late-night vulnerability matter more than all-day danger. Stay aware around ferry terminals and crowded commercial areas, use reputable drivers, and keep nightlife movement simple and group-based when possible.

neighborhoods.name: Masaki
neighborhoods.description: Upscale coastal area with restaurants, nightlife, and expat-friendly stays.

neighborhoods.name: Oyster Bay
neighborhoods.description: Popular district for cafés, embassies, and easier logistics.

neighborhoods.name: City Centre
neighborhoods.description: Busy core for ferries, markets, offices, and everyday city intensity.

mustVisit.name: National Museum of Tanzania
mustVisit.description: Museum with cultural and historical collections tied to the nation’s story.

mustVisit.name: Kariakoo Market
mustVisit.description: Massive market district for a vivid everyday-city experience.

mustVisit.name: Coco Beach
mustVisit.description: Casual shoreline hangout for sunsets, snacks, and local beach energy.

destinations.visaInfo: Visa or eVisa typically required for U.S. citizen visiting Tanzania; verify current entry rules before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: Swahili coast hub; Black-majority; vibrant community

destinations.tagline: Desert soul, deep history and a slower creative rhythm
destinations.vibe: Tucson feels grounded, artsy and unpretentious, with Sonoran food, mountain views and a pace that lets you breathe. For Black travelers, it is strongest when approached as a culture-and-landscape city, especially with stops that acknowledge Southern Arizona’s Black history.
destinations.safetyNotes: Central neighborhoods, the university area and well-traveled food corridors are the easiest zones for visitors. Tucson is typically more relaxed than Phoenix, but like many spread-out Sun Belt cities, deserted streets and car dependence can make parts of town feel isolated after dark; use regular urban caution and avoid wandering unfamiliar areas late at night.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Tucson
neighborhoods.description: Restored core with bars, art spaces and live music.

neighborhoods.name: Fourth Avenue
neighborhoods.description: Local strip of vintage shops, cafés and alternative energy.

neighborhoods.name: University Area
neighborhoods.description: Busy district around the University of Arizona with casual eats and museums.

mustVisit.name: African American Museum of Southern Arizona
mustVisit.description: Compact but meaningful museum preserving regional Black history.

mustVisit.name: Dunbar Pavilion

mustVisit.name: Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum
mustVisit.description: One of the region’s best nature-and-culture attractions.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen

destinations.tagline: Lake Victoria calm with western Kenya cultural pride.
destinations.vibe: Kisumu moves slower than Nairobi and feels more rooted in lakeside daily life, family networks, and regional culture. For Black travelers, it can feel pleasantly unhurried and more intimate, especially if you want local rhythm over big-city polish.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually easier to navigate than larger regional capitals, but petty theft, nightlife caution, and transport judgment still matter. Stick to known areas at night and arrange trusted rides rather than relying on guesswork after dark.

neighborhoods.name: Milimani
neighborhoods.description: Elevated, greener district with hotels, offices, and lake outlooks.

neighborhoods.name: CBD
neighborhoods.description: Central commercial area with markets, banks, and constant daytime activity.

neighborhoods.name: Dunga
neighborhoods.description: Lakeside area known for fish, wetlands, and sunset excursions.

mustVisit.name: Dunga Hill Camp
mustVisit.description: Known for sunset views, grilled fish, and easy Lake Victoria atmosphere.

mustVisit.name: Kisumu Museum
mustVisit.description: Regional museum highlighting Luo culture, ecology, and local history.

mustVisit.name: Impala Sanctuary
mustVisit.description: Small wildlife sanctuary close to town with walking-friendly grounds.

destinations.visaInfo: Electronic travel authorization typically required for U.S. citizen; verify current Kenya entry rules before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: Lake Victoria city; Black-majority western Kenya; welcoming

destinations.tagline: Quiet luxury and island calm under a volcanic peak
destinations.vibe: Nevis feels understated, green, and grown, more barefoot-villa than party island. For Black travelers, the island’s majority-Black Caribbean identity and slower pace make it especially easy to relax into.
destinations.safetyNotes: Nevis is generally calm and easygoing, with driving, road conditions, and beach isolation more relevant than crime for most visitors. Keep valuables out of sight in rental cars and use standard nighttime awareness around bars or empty roads.

neighborhoods.name: Charlestown
neighborhoods.description: Small capital with government buildings, history, and ferry access.

neighborhoods.name: Pinney's
neighborhoods.description: Beachside stretch known for resorts, bars, and sunset views.

neighborhoods.name: Newcastle

mustVisit.name: Pinney's Beach

mustVisit.name: Alexander Hamilton Museum

mustVisit.name: Nevis Peak / rainforest trails
mustVisit.description: Lush interior for hiking and a deeper look at the island beyond the shore.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays

destinations.tagline: Swahili coast history, beach days, and old-port energy.
destinations.vibe: Mombasa gives you layered coastal culture—Islamic, African, Indian Ocean, and port-city all at once. For Black travelers, it can feel rich and affirming culturally, especially when you lean into Swahili history instead of only resort life.
destinations.safetyNotes: Resort zones and well-trafficked historic areas are usually manageable, but theft and transport scams do happen and some coastal security concerns can shift quickly. Use reputable transport, be cautious at night, and monitor current advisories before going out far beyond core visitor areas.

neighborhoods.name: Old Town
neighborhoods.description: Historic quarter with carved doors, narrow streets, and deep Swahili-Arab heritage.

neighborhoods.name: Nyali
neighborhoods.description: Popular coastal district with beaches, malls, and many hotel options.

neighborhoods.name: Bamburi
neighborhoods.description: Resort-oriented area known for beach access and nightlife pockets.

mustVisit.name: Fort Jesus
mustVisit.description: UNESCO-listed fort that anchors the story of Mombasa’s coastal history.

mustVisit.name: Haller Park
mustVisit.description: Former quarry turned ecological park with walking paths and wildlife.

mustVisit.name: Mama Ngina Waterfront
mustVisit.description: Popular public waterfront for sea views, snacks, and evening city energy.

destinations.visaInfo: Electronic travel authorization typically required for U.S. citizen; verify current Kenya entry rules before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: Swahili coast; Black-majority; historical port city

destinations.tagline: Andean drama, colonial beauty, and a city under the volcanoes
destinations.vibe: Quito feels dramatic and elevated in every sense, with mountain air, layered history, and views that constantly remind you where you are. For Black travelers, it is best as a culture-and-scenery city where you pace yourself, respect the altitude, and build around the historic center plus a few smart modern-district stops.
destinations.safetyNotes: Quito can be very rewarding, but visitors should take urban safety seriously. Stick to known districts, use registered taxis or rideshare after dark, be cautious with phones in busy areas, and do not underestimate how quickly altitude plus a long day can make you less alert.

neighborhoods.name: Centro Historico
neighborhoods.description: UNESCO-listed core packed with churches, plazas, and big visual impact.

neighborhoods.name: La Floresta
neighborhoods.description: Creative district with cafés, culture spaces, and local hangouts.

neighborhoods.name: La Mariscal

mustVisit.name: Basilica del Voto Nacional
mustVisit.description: Dramatic landmark and one of the city’s most striking historic sites.

mustVisit.name: TeleferiQo
mustVisit.description: Cable-car experience with huge views over Quito and surrounding peaks.

mustVisit.name: Museo de la Ciudad
mustVisit.description: Solid museum stop for context on Quito’s history and identity.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen for short tourist stays
destinations.ratingNotes: Afro-Ecuadorian community in coast; discrimination in capital

destinations.tagline: Indonesia’s giant capital—fast, fashionable, and all business after dark.
destinations.vibe: Jakarta is huge, intense, and more interesting than pretty, with rooftop bars, serious food, and neighborhoods that change block by block. For Black travelers, it usually works best as a confidence city: move with a plan, pick your zones well, and let the food and nightlife do the talking.
destinations.safetyNotes: Mostly about urban caution—traffic, scams, nightlife judgment, and petty theft—rather than dramatic tourist danger. Stick to established districts, use app-based transport, and keep your phone and wallet close in crowded settings.

neighborhoods.name: SCBD/Sudirman
neighborhoods.description: Business core with upscale hotels, bars, and modern city polish.

neighborhoods.name: Kemang
neighborhoods.description: Expats, cafés, restaurants, and a more social residential feel.

neighborhoods.name: Kota Tua
neighborhoods.description: Historic old town with museums and colonial-era architecture.

mustVisit.name: National Monument (Monas)

mustVisit.name: Kota Tua Jakarta

mustVisit.name: Grand Indonesia

destinations.visaInfo: Visa-free or visa-on-arrival access may apply for U.S. citizen tourists depending on current Indonesia rules; verify before travel.

destinations.tagline: Jungle gateway with markets, Maya history, and adventure on deck
destinations.vibe: San Ignacio feels active, friendly, and easier to settle into than Belize City, with a nice blend of adventure travel and everyday town life. For Black travelers, it is one of the better Belize bases because it feels social and approachable without being all-resort.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally comfortable for visitors, though the usual small-town travel rules still apply after dark and on remote excursions. Book cave and river adventures with reputable operators and use normal caution with cash on market days.

neighborhoods.name: Town Center
neighborhoods.description: Walkable core with restaurants, tour offices, and the market.

neighborhoods.name: Santa Elena

neighborhoods.name: Macal River area

destinations.tagline: A giant, layered capital with culture in every direction
destinations.vibe: Mexico City can feel enormous at first, but it rewards travelers who move neighborhood by neighborhood instead of trying to conquer it all at once. For Black travelers, it shines through food, art, nightlife, and the ability to build a trip around stylish districts while still tapping into deeper history and a growing diaspora-aware scene.
destinations.safetyNotes: Mexico City is very doable with planning, but neighborhood choice matters a lot. Stay in well-trafficked areas like Roma, Condesa, or Polanco if you want ease, use rideshare at night, and be extra mindful of phones and bags in crowded markets, transit, and nightlife corridors.

neighborhoods.description: Tree-lined district with cafés, bars, galleries, and easy city energy.

neighborhoods.name: Centro Historico

destinations.tagline: Canals, culture, and easy bike-city flow.
destinations.vibe: Amsterdam is beautiful, compact, and easy to enjoy without overplanning, which makes it attractive for first-time Europe trips. For Black travelers, the city’s international feel helps, though nightlife and tourist-heavy areas can still bring the occasional ignorant interaction.
destinations.safetyNotes: Usually comfortable for visitors, with bikes, trams, and crowded nightlife zones posing more practical risk than personal danger. Watch for pickpocketing near Centraal, the Red Light District, and packed tram corridors.

neighborhoods.name: Jordaan
neighborhoods.description: Canal-lined district with boutiques, cafés, and postcard scenery.

neighborhoods.name: De Pijp

neighborhoods.name: Amsterdam Oost

destinations.tagline: Finger Lakes beauty with a thoughtful, artsy pulse
destinations.vibe: Ithaca feels progressive, bookish and outdoorsy, with enough Black history and small-business energy to make a short trip meaningful. From a Black traveler perspective, it is more low-key than hype, but the Southside story, Cornell influence and lake-country setting give it depth.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally comfortable for visitors, especially around downtown, Cornell and Collegetown, but it is still a small upstate city where quiet streets can feel isolated late at night. For Black travelers, the bigger concern is occasional microaggressions in less diverse spaces rather than overt hostility; rideshare or driving is smart after dark outside the core.

neighborhoods.description: Walkable core with restaurants, shops and the Commons.

destinations.tagline: Civil rights legacy with a strong everyday Black presence
destinations.vibe: Greensboro has that rare mix of movement history, HBCU energy and approachable city life. For Black travelers, it feels culturally legible and worth more than a quick stop because the sit-in legacy, arts scene and Black institutions all still shape the city’s identity.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown, the cultural center and HBCU-adjacent areas are generally straightforward for visitors. Greensboro tends to feel more balanced and welcoming than many peer cities, though nightlife and isolated streets still call for regular caution; the stronger issue is choosing the right base, not avoiding the city altogether.

destinations.tagline: A river city where Black memory and reinvention meet
destinations.vibe: Saginaw has a rough-around-the-edges feel, but there is real culture if you tap into its Black community history and local arts scene. It works best for travelers who value story, heritage, and neighborhood context over polished tourism.
destinations.safetyNotes: Saginaw rewards targeted travel more than spontaneous roaming. Stick to museum stops, downtown riverfront areas, and well-known districts, and use extra caution at night outside active corridors.

destinations.tagline: Progressive lakeside energy with real Black history under the surface
destinations.vibe: Madison feels clean, educated, and easy to enjoy, but it is most rewarding when you look beyond the postcard version of a college capital. For Black travelers, there is an interesting mix of progressive culture, Black history, and a city that is still figuring out how inclusive it truly feels on the ground.
destinations.safetyNotes: Madison is one of the smoother and more comfortable cities in this batch for visitors. Downtown, campus-adjacent areas, and East Side corridors are generally easy to navigate, though the city can still feel socially white in certain spaces.

destinations.tagline: A warm launchpad for Petra, café culture, and hilltop city views.
destinations.vibe: Amman feels layered and social, with Roman ruins, modern cafés, and a calmer pace than many regional capitals. For Black travelers, it can feel welcoming in hospitality settings, though modest dress and cultural awareness help you move more comfortably.
destinations.safetyNotes: Tourist areas in West Amman are generally manageable, but regional tensions mean you should stay alert, monitor advisories, and avoid demonstrations. Use trusted transport at night and be more cautious in less touristed eastern districts.

destinations.tagline: Black creativity with a live-music backbone
destinations.vibe: Austin can be energizing if you want music, food trucks, lake views, and neighborhoods with a strong creative pulse. From a Black traveler's perspective, the city feels best when you tap into East Austin's history, local festivals, and the Black-owned food and nightlife scene instead of treating it like only a tech playground.
destinations.safetyNotes: Austin is generally manageable for travelers, especially in central neighborhoods and major entertainment corridors, but nightlife zones can get rowdy late. Be intentional about transportation after dark, watch your belongings on busy weekends, and know that East Austin carries both deep Black history and ongoing gentrification tension.

destinations.tagline: Coming home. The warmth is real.
destinations.vibe: Coming home. The warmth is real and immediate.
destinations.safetyNotes: Ghana is one of the safest countries in West Africa and incredibly welcoming to the African diaspora. The Year of Return movement has made Black Americans feel like royalty here. Standard travel precautions apply: be aware of your belongings in markets, use trusted taxis or Uber, avoid displaying expensive electronics. Accra is safer than most major Western cities.

destinations.vibe: Albany (pronounced All-BENNY) was a key site of the Albany Movement — one of the earliest organized civil rights campaigns. Dr. King was arrested here. The Civil Rights Movement Museum is essential.
destinations.safetyNotes: Albany is a small southwest Georgia city. Downtown and civil rights sites are safe for visitors. A city with proud Black history.

destinations.safetyNotes: American Beach and Amelia Island are very safe. The beach community is quiet and historic — not a party destination but deeply significant.

destinations.tagline: Desert sprawl with standout food, art, and winter sun
destinations.vibe: Phoenix is most rewarding when you treat it like a metro of distinct pockets rather than one giant city. For a Black traveler, the trip works best through Roosevelt Row, South Phoenix history, Black-owned dining, and resort-style downtime instead of trying to do every suburb at once.
destinations.safetyNotes: Phoenix is workable for travelers but requires planning because the metro is large and car-dependent. Stick to well-trafficked districts, be cautious with nightlife parking lots and isolated desert trailheads, and be mindful of extreme heat as a real safety issue in warmer months.

destinations.vibe: Emerging Black travel destination with the powerful East Asheville and Eagle/Market Street district history. Asheville's reparations program for Black residents made national news. A progressive mountain city with stunning Blue Ridge scenery.
destinations.safetyNotes: Asheville is generally safe and progressive for a Southern city. Downtown and the River Arts District are very welcoming. The city has actively worked on racial equity initiatives.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Asheville

mustVisit.name: Black-owned restaurants on The Block

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens

destinations.tagline: The metro Atlanta area outside the city itself has significant Black community presence. Lithonia...
destinations.vibe: The metro Atlanta area outside the city itself has significant Black community presence. Lithonia, Conyers, and DeKalb County are home to one of the largest Black middle-class suburban populations in America.
destinations.safetyNotes: DeKalb County and the suburbs of Atlanta are generally very safe and have large, established Black communities. Standard suburban awareness applies.

neighborhoods.name: Lithonia / Stonecrest
neighborhoods.description: Stonecrest is an independent Black-majority city incorporated in 2017 — one of the largest majority-Black cities incorporated in decades

neighborhoods.name: Decatur
neighborhoods.description: Progressive suburb with excellent schools, diverse community, vibrant downtown

mustVisit.name: Stone Mountain Park
mustVisit.description: Complex history — Confederate carving on a mountain, but the park has beautiful trails, lakes, and activities

mustVisit.name: Stonecrest Mall area restaurants
mustVisit.description: Stonecrest has excellent Black-owned restaurants and the Crossing at Stonecrest

mustVisit.name: Arabia Mountain National Heritage Area
mustVisit.description: Stunning granite monadnocks near Lithonia — beautiful hiking and natural wildflowers

mustVisit.name: DeKalb Farmers Market
mustVisit.description: One of the most diverse markets in America — 100+ countries represented in produce and prepared foods

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: DeKalb County metro Atlanta; significant Black community presence

destinations.tagline: Home base for Black excellence
destinations.vibe: Home base for Black excellence — ATL is our city
destinations.safetyNotes: Atlanta is widely considered one of the most welcoming cities for Black travelers. Black Wall Street energy is alive in ATL. Standard city awareness applies — certain areas like Vine City at night, always check current conditions. Buckhead and Midtown are very safe. MARTA is reliable and safe during daytime.

neighborhoods.name: Old Fourth Ward
neighborhoods.description: MLK's birthplace neighborhood, now thriving with Black-owned restaurants and nightlife.

neighborhoods.name: West End
neighborhoods.description: Atlanta's historic Black neighborhood, home to AUC and deep community roots.

neighborhoods.name: Buckhead
neighborhoods.description: Upscale shopping and dining district, Atlanta's luxury zone.

neighborhoods.name: Midtown
neighborhoods.description: Arts, restaurants, and parks. Very walkable, very inclusive.

mustVisit.name: Sweet Auburn
mustVisit.description: MLK Jr.'s birthplace and the historic heart of Black Atlanta. The National Park site is free and deeply moving.

mustVisit.name: National Center for Civil and Human Rights
mustVisit.description: World-class museum connecting the American Civil Rights movement to global human rights struggles.
mustVisit.address: 100 Ivan Allen Jr Blvd NW, Atlanta, GA 30313

mustVisit.name: Busy Bee Cafe
mustVisit.description: Atlanta's oldest Black-owned soul food restaurant since 1947. The fried chicken is legendary.
mustVisit.address: 810 Martin Luther King Jr Dr SW, Atlanta, GA 30314

mustVisit.name: Cascade Road
mustVisit.description: Historic Black neighborhood known as 'Black Beverly Hills' — beautiful homes and deep community pride.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Black Mecca; vibrant Black community; enormous BOP ecosystem

destinations.tagline: Historic Black entertainment hub — Atlantic City's Kentucky Avenue was a 'mini Harlem' where Blac...
destinations.vibe: Historic Black entertainment hub — Atlantic City's Kentucky Avenue was a 'mini Harlem' where Black performers thrived when casinos were segregated. Still a popular weekend getaway for Black travelers from NYC, Philly, and DC.
destinations.safetyNotes: Atlantic City has significant economic challenges and elevated crime rates. Stay on the Boardwalk and casino areas. The Kentucky Avenue historic district is safe to visit during the day. The Atlantic Ocean beaches are free and accessible.

neighborhoods.name: The Boardwalk
neighborhoods.description: America's first boardwalk — historic entertainment strip

neighborhoods.name: Chelsea / Inlet
neighborhoods.description: African American community neighborhoods

neighborhoods.name: Kentucky Avenue Historic District
neighborhoods.description: Once the Black entertainment strip — Club Harlem, Northside hotels for Black performers

mustVisit.name: African American Heritage Museum of Southern New Jersey
mustVisit.description: Documents the rich African American history of the South Jersey/Atlantic City region

mustVisit.name: Kentucky Avenue historic area
mustVisit.description: Walk where Ella Fitzgerald, Louis Armstrong, and Ray Charles performed for Black audiences

mustVisit.name: Atlantic City Casinos
mustVisit.description: Borgata, Hard Rock, Caesars — major entertainment destinations

mustVisit.name: Atlantic City Beach
mustVisit.description: Free public beach with views of the Boardwalk — popular with Black travelers in summer

mustVisit.name: Revel Beach / Resorts Beach
mustVisit.description: Multiple beach access points along the Boardwalk

destinations.bestTimeToVisit: May-September (beach season), year-round for casino visits
destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Historic Black entertainment hub; Kentucky Ave legacy; declining

destinations.tagline: The Bahamas is an easy getaway from Miami — 3-hour flight or even a ferry. Crystal clear turquois...
destinations.vibe: The Bahamas is an easy getaway from Miami — 3-hour flight or even a ferry. Crystal clear turquoise water, Atlantis resort, and a largely Black population make Nassau very welcoming for Black American travelers.
destinations.safetyNotes: Nassau is very tourist-friendly and the Bahamas has a predominantly Black population. Tourist areas like Cable Beach, Paradise Island (Atlantis), and downtown Nassau are safe. Some areas of Nassau require standard urban awareness.

neighborhoods.name: Paradise Island / Atlantis
neighborhoods.description: World-famous mega-resort with waterpark, casino, and beach access

neighborhoods.name: Cable Beach
neighborhoods.description: Main hotel beach area — beautiful stretch of white sand

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Nassau
neighborhoods.description: British colonial architecture, straw market, local culture and history

neighborhoods.name: Exuma / Out Islands
neighborhoods.description: Pig Beach and swimming pigs — unique Bahamas experience on Exuma island

mustVisit.name: Atlantis Paradise Island
mustVisit.description: Iconic mega-resort with waterpark, casino, and aquarium — a world unto itself

mustVisit.name: Cabbage Beach / Atlantis Beach
mustVisit.description: Stunning pink and white sand beach on Paradise Island

mustVisit.name: Exuma (Swimming Pigs)
mustVisit.description: Famous swimming pigs of Pig Beach on Exuma — a surreal only-in-Bahamas experience

mustVisit.name: Clifton Heritage National Park
mustVisit.description: Documents Lucayan, African, and British heritage of the Bahamas

mustVisit.name: Nassau Straw Market
