destinations.bestTimeToVisit: April-June, September-November (avoid summer heat and packed bachelorette crowds)
destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Growing Black community; Fisk HBCU; gentrification changing city

destinations.tagline: Before the Civil War, Natchez had the highest concentration of millionaires in America — built on...
destinations.vibe: Before the Civil War, Natchez had the highest concentration of millionaires in America — built on enslaved labor. The Natchez slaves' story is critical American history. Forks of the Road slave market site is here.
destinations.safetyNotes: Natchez is a small, charming Mississippi town. Downtown and the bluffs are safe for visitors. A complex history of slavery presented at various sites.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Natchez / The Bluffs
neighborhoods.description: Antebellum mansions built on enslaved labor — complex history being reckoned with

neighborhoods.name: Under the Hill
neighborhoods.description: Historic port area on the Mississippi River

mustVisit.name: Forks of the Road Historic Site
mustVisit.description: Second-largest slave market in the US — Mississippi Freedom Trail site

mustVisit.name: Natchez National Historical Park
mustVisit.description: Documents Natchez's complex history including William Johnson, a free Black barber who kept a remarkable diary

mustVisit.name: Melrose Estate
mustVisit.description: Plantation house with interpreted history of the enslaved community

mustVisit.name: The Natchez Museum of African American History & Culture
mustVisit.description: Documents African American heritage in the Natchez area

mustVisit.name: The Magnolia Hotel
mustVisit.description: Historic hotel in downtown Natchez

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Ante-bellum history; significant Black heritage; Mississippi community

destinations.tagline: Cajun Country and the Creole heartland — Lafayette is the hub of Zydeco music, Creole cuisine, an...
destinations.vibe: Cajun Country and the Creole heartland — Lafayette is the hub of Zydeco music, Creole cuisine, and Black Louisiana culture. Mardi Gras celebrations here are more authentic and community-focused than New Orleans.
destinations.safetyNotes: Lafayette and the Cajun Country region are generally safe and welcoming. The vibrant Creole and Cajun communities enthusiastically welcome all visitors.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Lafayette
neighborhoods.description: Zydeco music epicenter, excellent Creole restaurants, Festivals Acadiens

neighborhoods.name: Breaux Bridge
neighborhoods.description: Crawfish Festival, Zydeco on Saturdays at Café des Amis

mustVisit.name: Zydeco music performances
mustVisit.description: Clifton Chenier invented Zydeco — see live performances at Slim's Y-Ki-Ki in Opelousas

mustVisit.name: Prejean's Restaurant
mustVisit.description: The best of Cajun cuisine in Lafayette — crawfish étouffée and live Cajun music

mustVisit.name: Vermilionville Heritage Village
mustVisit.description: Living history museum documenting Creole and Cajun culture 1765-1890

mustVisit.name: Lafayette Mardi Gras
mustVisit.description: More family-friendly than New Orleans — courir de Mardi Gras on horseback

mustVisit.name: Breaux Bridge Crawfish Festival (May)
mustVisit.description: Two-day festival celebrating crawfish — Zydeco bands, crawfish eating contests, family fun

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Creole heartland; Zydeco; Black Cajun culture significant

destinations.tagline: The most African city in America
destinations.vibe: The most African city in America — jazz, second lines, and Creole soul.
destinations.safetyNotes: New Orleans has a strong Black community that is warm and proud. The French Quarter and Garden District are very tourist-safe. Tremé and Seventh Ward are historic Black neighborhoods worth visiting — use common sense at night. Avoid wandering unfamiliar side streets after midnight. Uber is reliable. The energy during Mardi Gras and Jazz Fest is electrifying but crowds require extra awareness of your belongings.

neighborhoods.name: Tremé
neighborhoods.description: The oldest Black neighborhood in America. Birthplace of jazz, home to second line culture, and pure New Orleans soul.

neighborhoods.name: French Quarter
neighborhoods.description: Iconic architecture, live jazz on every corner, Bourbon Street energy, and world-class Creole restaurants.

neighborhoods.name: Garden District
neighborhoods.description: Beautiful antebellum mansions, oak-lined streets, and upscale restaurants. Great for brunch and walking.

neighborhoods.name: Frenchmen Street
neighborhoods.description: The locals' alternative to Bourbon Street — live music every night, great bars, authentic NOLA vibes.

mustVisit.name: Dooky Chase Restaurant
mustVisit.description: The Queen of Creole cuisine. Leah Chase fed civil rights leaders here. An absolute institution of Black New Orleans.

mustVisit.name: New Orleans African American Museum
mustVisit.description: Located in Tremé, this museum tells the rich story of New Orleans Black history from free people of color to today.
mustVisit.address: 1418 Governor Nicholls St, New Orleans, LA 70116

mustVisit.name: Second Line Parade
mustVisit.description: The most joyful tradition in Black America. A jazz funeral procession turned street party — check local schedules for Social Aid & Pleasure Club second lines.

mustVisit.name: Preservation Hall
mustVisit.description: Legendary jazz venue in the French Quarter. Small, intimate, and absolutely sacred. Shows sell out — book ahead.
mustVisit.address: 726 St Peter St, New Orleans, LA 70116

destinations.bestTimeToVisit: October-May (avoid summer heat and hurricane season)
destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Most African US city; Tremé; jazz culture; Black hospitality embedded

destinations.tagline: Harlem is everywhere — the whole world catches up
destinations.vibe: Harlem is the cultural capital of Black America. New York is our proving ground.
destinations.safetyNotes: New York City is one of the safest large cities in the world and extremely diverse. Harlem is now very safe and gentrified — the community is still vibrant but evolving. Brooklyn's Bed-Stuy and Crown Heights are thriving Black neighborhoods. The subway is safe during daytime; be alert late nights. Standard city awareness applies.

neighborhoods.name: Harlem
neighborhoods.description: The cultural capital of Black America. 125th Street, the Apollo Theater, soul food restaurants, and living history on every block.

neighborhoods.name: Bed-Stuy, Brooklyn
neighborhoods.description: Bedford-Stuyvesant is Brooklyn's Black heart — brownstones, Caribbean food, Black-owned boutiques, and deep community pride.

neighborhoods.name: Crown Heights, Brooklyn
neighborhoods.description: Strong Caribbean community — Jamaican and Trinidadian restaurants, the West Indian Day Parade, and beautiful brownstones.

neighborhoods.name: Bronx
neighborhoods.description: Birthplace of hip-hop. The Universal Hip Hop Museum is here. Real, authentic borough energy.

mustVisit.name: Apollo Theater
mustVisit.description: The most famous stage in Black entertainment history. Amateur Night is a rite of passage. Check their calendar for events.

mustVisit.name: Museum of African American Art
mustVisit.description: Harlem's gem — rotating exhibitions of African American fine art and history in a stunning space.

mustVisit.name: Sylvia's Restaurant
mustVisit.description: Harlem's most famous soul food restaurant since 1962. A Sunday gospel brunch here is a bucket-list experience.
mustVisit.address: 328 Malcolm X Blvd, New York, NY 10027

mustVisit.name: Universal Hip Hop Museum
mustVisit.description: Dedicated to preserving hip-hop culture born right here in the Bronx. An essential stop for any culture lover.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Large Black community; NYPD history; diverse neighborhoods

destinations.tagline: More than football, with quiet layers of Black history
destinations.vibe: Green Bay is best known for the Packers, but the city also offers waterfront calm, small-city friendliness, and lesser-known Black history. For Black travelers, it is more of a comfortable Midwest stop than a culture capital, but it has enough local texture to be worthwhile.
destinations.safetyNotes: Green Bay is one of the easier cities in this batch to navigate from a visitor-safety standpoint. Main tourism areas feel straightforward, though like many small cities it gets quieter early and nightlife pockets are limited.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Green Bay
neighborhoods.description: Riverfront core with restaurants, walkable blocks, and event energy.

neighborhoods.name: Titletown District
neighborhoods.description: Modern development near Lambeau with hotels, food, and entertainment.

neighborhoods.name: Astor Historic District
neighborhoods.description: Historic residential area with older homes and quieter streets.

mustVisit.name: Lambeau Field
mustVisit.description: Must-see even for non-fans because it defines the city's identity.

mustVisit.name: National Railroad Museum
mustVisit.description: Large museum stop that makes Green Bay feel broader than game-day tourism.

mustVisit.name: Neville Public Museum
mustVisit.description: Local museum covering history, science, and regional culture.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Predominantly white Wisconsin; beyond Packers limited Black culture

destinations.tagline: Rust Belt resilience with culture, parks and comeback energy
destinations.vibe: Youngstown feels gritty, proud and in transition, with an arts scene and park system that give the city more range than outsiders expect. For Black travelers, it works best as a revival-story destination where you pair local history with cultural stops and keep expectations grounded but open.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown, the university area and formal attractions are the best bases for visitors. Youngstown rewards awareness more than spontaneity, especially after dark, and Black travelers may prefer planning around known venues and daytime sightseeing rather than treating the city like a free-roaming nightlife destination.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Youngstown
neighborhoods.description: Compact center with performance venues, food and civic spaces.

neighborhoods.name: Wick Park / University Area
neighborhoods.description: Historic homes and YSU-adjacent institutions near the core.

neighborhoods.name: Mill Creek Corridor
neighborhoods.description: Green, scenic zone connected to the city’s signature park system.

mustVisit.name: The Butler Institute of American Art
mustVisit.description: Major free art museum and one of Youngstown’s strongest attractions.

mustVisit.name: Youngstown Historical Center of Industry & Labor
mustVisit.description: Best place to understand the city’s steel-era identity.

mustVisit.name: Mill Creek Park
mustVisit.description: Huge urban park that gives Youngstown real outdoor appeal.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Rust Belt; majority-Black neighborhoods; economic depression

destinations.tagline: Modern architecture and port-city cool without the tourist swarm.
destinations.vibe: Rotterdam feels more contemporary and multicultural than Amsterdam, with a little less performance and a little more local rhythm. Black travelers often appreciate its diversity and everyday-city energy, especially if they prefer design, food, and neighborhoods over postcard canals.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally comfortable, with standard big-city caution needed around stations and late-night nightlife clusters. The city is easier to move through than many capitals, but busy transport hubs still attract petty theft.

neighborhoods.name: Centrum
neighborhoods.description: Modern core with landmark architecture and transit convenience.

neighborhoods.name: Witte de Withkwartier
neighborhoods.description: Cultural strip with galleries, bars, and creative city energy.

neighborhoods.name: Kop van Zuid
neighborhoods.description: Redeveloped waterfront district with skyline views and upscale stays.

mustVisit.name: Markthal
mustVisit.description: Iconic indoor market hall with food stalls and bold design.

mustVisit.name: Depot Boijmans Van Beuningen
mustVisit.description: Distinctive mirrored art-storage museum experience.

mustVisit.name: Erasmus Bridge
mustVisit.description: Signature bridge and a defining piece of the city skyline.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays in the Netherlands/Schengen.
destinations.ratingNotes: More diverse than Amsterdam; Surinamese community; still racism

destinations.tagline: Horse-country elegance with overlooked Black heritage
destinations.vibe: Aiken is polished and understated, known for equestrian culture, gardens and easy Southern pacing. For Black travelers, the city gets more interesting when you move beyond its genteel image and spend time with the Black educational and cultural history tied to the Immanuel Institute and surrounding community.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown and the main visitor corridors are usually comfortable and manageable. The bigger Black-traveler consideration here is not danger so much as social contrast in a traditionally elite-feeling destination; move with normal small-city caution and build your itinerary around the spots that show the fuller story of Aiken.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Aiken
neighborhoods.description: Compact center with restaurants, shops and civic landmarks.

neighborhoods.name: South Boundary Area
neighborhoods.description: Tree-lined district known for stately homes and classic Aiken charm.

neighborhoods.name: Immanuel Area
neighborhoods.description: Historic area connected to Black education and community life.

mustVisit.name: Center for African American History, Art and Culture
mustVisit.description: Key stop for learning Aiken’s Black educational and community history.

mustVisit.name: Hopelands Gardens
mustVisit.description: Beautiful public gardens that fit Aiken’s graceful atmosphere.

mustVisit.name: Aiken Thoroughbred Racing Hall of Fame
mustVisit.description: Good cultural stop in a city shaped by equestrian traditions.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Horse country SC; limited Black community infrastructure

destinations.tagline: Birthplace of the Black Panther Party, deep Afrocentric culture, and one of the most politically ...
destinations.vibe: Birthplace of the Black Panther Party, deep Afrocentric culture, and one of the most politically conscious Black communities in America. Oakland's vibrant arts scene, Black-owned restaurants, and Fruitvale district make it essential.
destinations.safetyNotes: Oakland has a mixed safety reputation. Uptown Oakland, Temescal, Fruitvale, and Jack London Square are generally safe for visitors. Some neighborhoods have higher crime. The Black Panther history tour is perfectly safe.

neighborhoods.name: Uptown Oakland
neighborhoods.description: Fox Theater, Black Panther murals, heart of Black Oakland culture

neighborhoods.name: West Oakland
neighborhoods.description: Birth of the Black Panther Party — Huey Newton and Bobby Seale's ground

neighborhoods.name: Fruitvale
neighborhoods.description: Vibrant community, site of Oscar Grant tragedy, murals and culture

neighborhoods.name: Temescal
neighborhoods.description: Excellent Black-owned restaurants and businesses amid rapid change

mustVisit.name: African American Museum and Library at Oakland
mustVisit.description: Free museum with extensive archives on California's Black history

mustVisit.name: Black Panther Party Historic Sites
mustVisit.description: Self-guided tour of West Oakland — Bobby Hutton Memorial Park, HQ sites

mustVisit.name: Everett and Jones Barbeque
mustVisit.description: Bay Area BBQ legend — Black-owned institution since 1973

mustVisit.name: Bird & Beckett Books
mustVisit.description: Independent bookstore hosting jazz performances and community events

mustVisit.name: Lake Merritt
mustVisit.description: Urban lake popular for picnics, kayaking, walking — heart of Oakland outdoor culture

mustVisit.name: Oakstop / Black Arts Movement
mustVisit.description: Hub for Black arts and culture programming in Oakland

destinations.bestTimeToVisit: April-October (San Francisco Bay weather is mild year-round, summers are great)
destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Black Panther birthplace; strong Afrocentric culture; BOP solid

destinations.tagline: Theme park capital of the world with a huge African American tourism market. FAMU's historic conn...
destinations.vibe: Theme park capital of the world with a huge African American tourism market. FAMU's historic connection to Central Florida and a growing Black-owned business scene complement the Disney/Universal draw.
destinations.safetyNotes: Orlando's tourist corridor (International Drive, Disney, Universal) is very safe and family-friendly. Downtown Orlando requires standard urban awareness.

neighborhoods.name: Parramore
neighborhoods.description: Historic Black Orlando — facing gentrification pressures, community organizing

neighborhoods.name: International Drive
neighborhoods.description: Hotel corridor and entertainment hub

neighborhoods.name: Lake Nona
neighborhoods.description: Growing diverse suburban community

mustVisit.name: Walt Disney World
mustVisit.description: The classic — notably popular for Black families, especially Magic Kingdom and EPCOT

mustVisit.name: Universal Studios
mustVisit.description: Harry Potter, Minions, and more — huge with Black families

mustVisit.name: Wells'Built Museum of African American History
mustVisit.description: Historic Black hotel and cultural center — documents Orlando's African American history

mustVisit.name: Dr. Phillips Center for Performing Arts
mustVisit.description: World-class performances including touring Broadway and concerts

mustVisit.name: ICON Park
mustVisit.description: Observation wheel and entertainment complex on International Drive

mustVisit.name: Shake Shack, Baoery, and local Orlando dining
mustVisit.description: Orlando's restaurant scene is rapidly growing

destinations.bestTimeToVisit: October-April (avoid summer heat and peak tourist season), Christmas season is spectacular
destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Theme park hub; large Black tourist market; FAMU connection

destinations.tagline: North Carolina's Outer Banks has Roanoke Island's Freedmen's Colony history and beautiful beaches...
destinations.vibe: North Carolina's Outer Banks has Roanoke Island's Freedmen's Colony history and beautiful beaches. Popular summer destination for Black families from the mid-Atlantic and Southeast.
destinations.safetyNotes: Outer Banks is generally safe but predominantly white. Some Black travelers report feeling like the only person of color. However the beaches are beautiful and the history is significant.

neighborhoods.name: Roanoke Island / Manteo
neighborhoods.description: Site of the Civil War Freedmen's Colony — 3,000 formerly enslaved people lived here

neighborhoods.name: Nags Head / Kitty Hawk
neighborhoods.description: Wright Brothers first flight, beach rentals

neighborhoods.name: Ocracoke
neighborhoods.description: Beautiful remote island accessible by ferry — quieter alternative

mustVisit.name: Freedmen's Colony of Roanoke Island
mustVisit.description: Civil War-era colony where 3,000 freed Black people built a self-sustaining community — interpretive trail available

mustVisit.name: Cape Hatteras National Seashore
mustVisit.description: Pristine national seashore with beautiful beaches and the famous striped lighthouse

mustVisit.name: Wright Brothers National Memorial
mustVisit.description: Where human flight began — Kill Devil Hills

mustVisit.name: Jockey's Ridge State Park
mustVisit.description: Largest natural living sand dune on East Coast — hang gliding and sandboarding

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Predominantly white coastal NC; Roanoke history but limited infrastructure

destinations.tagline: Spring break capital with a significant Black college spring break tradition. Emerald Coast beach...
destinations.vibe: Spring break capital with a significant Black college spring break tradition. Emerald Coast beaches draw Black travelers from across the Southeast. One of the top Florida beach destinations.
destinations.safetyNotes: Panama City Beach is a spring break town — safety varies significantly by season and event. BCBF (Black College Spring Break Festival) is held here annually and city welcomes Black visitors.

neighborhoods.name: Front Beach Road
neighborhoods.description: Main strip with clubs, restaurants, and direct beach access

neighborhoods.name: Pier Park
neighborhoods.description: Major outdoor shopping and dining complex

mustVisit.name: PCB Beaches
mustVisit.description: Stunning emerald-green Gulf Coast waters with white sand — some of Florida's best beaches

mustVisit.name: Black College Spring Break (March)
mustVisit.description: Annual gathering of HBCU students — beach parties, step shows, concerts

mustVisit.name: Pier Park
mustVisit.description: Major outdoor shopping complex with restaurants and entertainment

mustVisit.name: Gulf World Marine Park
mustVisit.description: Dolphin shows and marine exhibits popular with families

mustVisit.name: St. Andrews State Park
mustVisit.description: Beautiful unspoiled beach and nature area on the Gulf Coast

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Black college spring break tradition; beach access

destinations.tagline: Black Americans have always found freedom in Paris
destinations.vibe: Black Americans have always found freedom in Paris — from Josephine Baker to James Baldwin, the city embraces us.
destinations.safetyNotes: Paris is generally safe for tourists, especially in the central arrondissements. The Black American tradition of finding liberation in Paris is real — you will feel less hypervisible here than in many American cities. Pickpockets operate around major tourist sites (Eiffel Tower, Montmartre) — use a money belt. Paris has a significant African and Caribbean community, especially in the northern arrondissements (18th, 19th).

neighborhoods.name: Montmartre
neighborhoods.description: Hilltop village with artist history — the Sacre-Coeur, jazz clubs, and bohemian energy that attracted Baldwin and Baker.

neighborhoods.name: Le Marais
neighborhoods.description: Paris's most fashionable district — excellent museums, falafel on Rue des Rosiers, galleries, and vibrant nightlife.

neighborhoods.name: Chateau Rouge (18th)
neighborhoods.description: The African heart of Paris — Cameroonian, Senegalese, and Congolese markets, food, and community. Africa in Europe.

neighborhoods.name: Saint-Germain-des-Pres
neighborhoods.description: Where James Baldwin wrote and Richard Wright lived — jazz cafes, literary history, and Parisian elegance.

mustVisit.name: Josephine Baker's Paris
mustVisit.description: Follow in the footsteps of the legend — visit the Folies Bergere where she became a star, and see her honorary tomb at the Pantheon.

mustVisit.name: Musee du quai Branly
mustVisit.description: World-class museum of African, Asian, Oceanian and Americas indigenous art. One of the best African art collections in the world.

mustVisit.name: Chateau Rouge Market
mustVisit.description: Explore the African neighborhood in the 18th — plantain, piment, African fabrics, and the most authentic African food in Paris.

mustVisit.name: New Morning Jazz Club
mustVisit.description: Legendary Paris jazz club that has hosted Miles Davis and Chet Baker. Still one of the best jazz venues in Europe.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens (Schengen — 90 days max)
destinations.ratingNotes: Black Americans' historic refuge; large African diaspora; some racism

destinations.tagline: Pensacola Beach is one of Florida's most beautiful beaches with sugar-white sand. The city has si...
destinations.vibe: Pensacola Beach is one of Florida's most beautiful beaches with sugar-white sand. The city has significant African American history as the birthplace of Andrew Young. The Tuskegee Airmen connection at NAS Pensacola is significant.
destinations.safetyNotes: Pensacola Beach and tourist areas are very safe. Pensacola proper is a mid-size city with standard awareness needed in some neighborhoods. Military city — generally conservative but welcoming to tourists.

neighborhoods.name: Pensacola Beach
neighborhoods.description: Pristine white sand Gulf Coast beach on Santa Rosa Island

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Pensacola
neighborhoods.description: Seville Historic District — one of Florida's oldest settlements

mustVisit.name: Pensacola Beach
mustVisit.description: Stunning quartz-white sand and emerald Gulf water — consistently ranked Florida's best beach

mustVisit.name: National Museum of Naval Aviation (NAS Pensacola)
mustVisit.description: Tuskegee Airmen exhibit at the nation's premier naval aviation museum — free and powerful

mustVisit.name: Gulf Islands National Seashore
mustVisit.description: Pristine national seashore with uncrowded beaches and Civil War-era forts

mustVisit.name: T.T. Wentworth Jr. Florida State Museum
mustVisit.description: Florida history museum in Pensacola's historic district

destinations.bestTimeToVisit: April-October (beach season), avoid hurricane season peaks
destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens
destinations.ratingNotes: Northwest Florida; military town; limited Black infrastructure

destinations.tagline: Home to the oldest African American church (Mother Bethel AME), a thriving Black arts scene, and ...
destinations.vibe: Home to the oldest African American church (Mother Bethel AME), a thriving Black arts scene, and rich civil rights history. West Philadelphia's Black community is culturally powerful.
destinations.safetyNotes: Philadelphia has some high-crime neighborhoods but tourist and cultural areas are generally safe. North Philly requires more awareness. West Philly, South Philly, and Center City are accessible for visitors.

neighborhoods.name: West Philadelphia
neighborhoods.description: Strong African American community, University City, HBCU-adjacent culture

neighborhoods.name: North Philadelphia
neighborhoods.description: Historically significant Black community, murals, community organizations

neighborhoods.name: South Philadelphia

neighborhoods.name: Germantown

mustVisit.name: Odunde Festival

destinations.bestTimeToVisit: April-October (winters are harsh), summer festivals are excellent

destinations.tagline: Pittsburgh's Hill District was a major Great Migration destination — August Wilson's plays are se...
destinations.vibe: Pittsburgh's Hill District was a major Great Migration destination — August Wilson's plays are set here. The August Wilson African American Cultural Center makes Pittsburgh an emerging Black travel destination.
destinations.safetyNotes: Pittsburgh is generally safe in tourist areas. Downtown, the Strip District, and South Side are very accessible. The Hill District is safe to visit for cultural sites during the day.

destinations.tagline: More sophisticated and artsy than Cancn, Playa del Carmen is beloved by Black travelers seeking a...
destinations.vibe: More sophisticated and artsy than Cancn, Playa del Carmen is beloved by Black travelers seeking authenticity with Caribbean beauty. La Casa de la Playa won Travel Noire's 2024 Best All-Inclusive award. The 5th Avenue pedestrian strip is legendary.
destinations.safetyNotes: Playa del Carmen is generally very safe in tourist zones. 5th Avenue and beach areas are well-patrolled. Exercise standard awareness away from tourist corridors. Generally very Black-friendly with a cosmopolitan vibe.

neighborhoods.description: The vibrant pedestrian spine of Playa — shopping, dining, and nightlife

neighborhoods.name: Playacar

destinations.tagline: Despite Oregon's history of Black exclusion laws, Portland has a significant Black community in N...
destinations.vibe: Despite Oregon's history of Black exclusion laws, Portland has a significant Black community in Northeast Portland and the Albina District. Portland's 100+ consecutive nights of George Floyd protests showed the community's commitment to racial justice. Growing Black restaurant scene.
destinations.safetyNotes: Portland is generally safe in tourist areas (Pearl District, NW Portland). Some areas of the city have had elevated social challenges post-2020. Northeast Portland's Black community is safe and welcoming.

destinations.tagline: US territory — no passport needed for Americans. Puerto Rico's Afro-Boricua culture, beautiful be...
destinations.vibe: US territory — no passport needed for Americans. Puerto Rico's Afro-Boricua culture, beautiful beaches, El Yunque rainforest, and Old San Juan make it one of the top Caribbean destinations for Black Americans.
destinations.safetyNotes: Old San Juan and tourist areas are very safe. San Juan's Condado and Isla Verde are upscale beach hotel zones. Parts of San Juan require standard urban awareness. Puerto Rico is a US territory — same travel protections apply.

neighborhoods.name: Old San Juan

destinations.tagline: Puerto Vallarta is the more authentic, culturally rich alternative to Cancn on Mexico's Pacific c...
destinations.vibe: Puerto Vallarta is the more authentic, culturally rich alternative to Cancn on Mexico's Pacific coast. Popular with Black LGBTQ+ travelers and those seeking a more Mexican cultural experience beyond the all-inclusive bubble.
destinations.safetyNotes: Puerto Vallarta is one of Mexico's safest tourist destinations. The Hotel Zone, Romantic Zone, and Centro are very safe for tourists. US State Department advisory has PV in a manageable safety tier.

neighborhoods.name: Zona Romntica (Romantic Zone)

neighborhoods.name: Centro Histrico

mustVisit.name: La Palapa Restaurant

destinations.tagline: The Dominican Republic is the most visited country in the Caribbean. Punta Cana's all-inclusive r...
destinations.vibe: The Dominican Republic is the most visited country in the Caribbean. Punta Cana's all-inclusive resorts, beautiful beaches, and Afro-Dominican culture make it extremely popular with Black American travelers.
destinations.safetyNotes: Punta Cana resorts are extremely safe — resort hotels are essentially all-inclusive compounds. Outside resorts in local areas, standard travel awareness applies. Some concerns about water and food safety led to resort filtering systems being standard.

neighborhoods.name: Punta Cana Resort Area (Bvaro)

neighborhoods.name: Cap Cana
neighborhoods.description: High-end gated resort community with private beaches

neighborhoods.description: Colonial Zone — oldest continuously inhabited European city in Americas, Afro-Dominican culture

mustVisit.name: Bvaro Beach
mustVisit.description: One of the most beautiful beaches in the Caribbean — calm turquoise water and white sand

mustVisit.name: Saona Island Day Trip

mustVisit.name: Hoyo Azul (Scape Park)

destinations.tagline: Creative Triad city with deep African American roots
destinations.vibe: Winston-Salem blends arts-district polish with a substantial Black history that is easier to feel once you get beyond the usual Moravian storyline. For Black travelers, the city can be genuinely rewarding because its historic neighborhoods, Black arts spaces and HBCU ties give it texture.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown, arts districts and planned heritage stops are the easiest places to explore. The city is usually comfortable for visitors, but like many mid-sized Southern cities, some blocks empty out fast after dark, so the best move is to keep your nightlife clustered and transportation simple.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown Arts District
neighborhoods.description: Creative core with galleries, restaurants and public art.

neighborhoods.name: Old Salem
neighborhoods.description: Historic district central to the city’s early identity.

neighborhoods.name: Depot Street Area
neighborhoods.description: Historic Black neighborhood tied to African American community life.

mustVisit.name: Historic Depot Street Tour

mustVisit.name: Old Salem Museums & Gardens
mustVisit.description: Signature city attraction with broader historical context.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: HBCU cluster; Black Sankofa culture; growing arts scene

destinations.tagline: Finance-meets-beach luxury with crystal water and easy logistics
destinations.vibe: Grand Cayman feels polished, organized, and expensive, with a smoother practical experience than many islands. For Black travelers, it is best when you want clear water, food, and comfort first, with local culture layered in intentionally.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally one of the easier Caribbean destinations for visitors, with reliable infrastructure and low-stress resort areas. Still watch belongings at beaches and in rental cars, and be mindful when driving on the left.

neighborhoods.name: Seven Mile Beach

neighborhoods.name: George Town

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for U.S. citizen for short tourist stays
destinations.ratingNotes: Black-majority but finance hub; welcoming but luxury-oriented

destinations.tagline: Diaspora energy between mountains and the Pacific
destinations.vibe: Vancouver feels polished, outdoorsy, and global, with neighborhoods that shift from waterfront luxury to deeply local food scenes fast. For Black travelers, it lands best when you lean into the city’s multicultural rhythm, Caribbean and African dining, and a trip style that balances scenery with intentional neighborhood hopping.
destinations.safetyNotes: Vancouver is generally comfortable for visitors, especially in major neighborhoods and along the seawall. The main adjustment is being mindful around parts of Downtown Eastside, staying aware late at night, and not confusing the city’s overall calm feel with a total lack of street-level issues.

neighborhoods.name: Downtown / Coal Harbour
neighborhoods.description: Central zone for hotels, skyline views, and easy seawall access.

neighborhoods.name: Commercial Drive
neighborhoods.description: Eclectic east-side strip known for independent shops and diverse food.

neighborhoods.name: Kitsilano
neighborhoods.description: Beach-adjacent neighborhood with cafés, wellness spots, and relaxed style.

mustVisit.name: Granville Island Public Market
mustVisit.description: Food-and-art hub that gives a strong local flavor of the city.

mustVisit.name: Museum of Anthropology
mustVisit.description: Major museum experience centered on Indigenous art and histories.

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizen
destinations.ratingNotes: Pacific diaspora; diverse; welcoming; growing Black community

destinations.tagline: Texas culture, Black heritage, and one of the South's most usable city getaways
destinations.vibe: San Antonio gives you history, food, nightlife, and a much stronger Black cultural story than many visitors realize. For Black travelers, the city feels welcoming when you move beyond the River Walk and include the East Side, Carver legacy, and Black heritage experiences.
destinations.safetyNotes: San Antonio is one of the more visitor-friendly cities in this batch, especially around the River Walk, Pearl, and major museum areas. Use normal big-city caution after dark and be selective with nightlife zones outside the main corridors.

mustVisit.name: San Antonio River Walk
mustVisit.description: Classic city attraction that still belongs in a balanced itinerary.

destinations.safetyNotes: Raleigh is generally very safe. Downtown, Glenwood South, and Five Points are accessible. Durham's Hayti district nearby is historically significant.

destinations.tagline: West Texas grit with meaningful Black heritage stops
destinations.vibe: Lubbock is more practical than glamorous, but it has enough music, museum, and Black heritage value to justify a targeted stop. For Black travelers, East Lubbock history and the Caviel Museum give the city more substance than a surface-level visit would suggest.
destinations.safetyNotes: Lubbock is generally workable for visitors, especially if you keep to known districts and use a car. It is not a place for dense pedestrian tourism, so comfort depends a lot on planning your routes and stops.

destinations.safetyNotes: Rehoboth Beach is very safe and welcoming. One of the most LGBTQ+ friendly beach towns on the East Coast. Generally very diverse and inclusive.

destinations.tagline: Former capital of the Confederacy transformed into a city reckoning with its history. Home to the...

destinations.safetyNotes: Richmond is generally safe in tourist areas. Jackson Ward, the Fan, Shockoe Bottom, and Scott's Addition are accessible. The city is actively confronting its Confederate monument history.

destinations.tagline: Luxury desert escape with nightlife and spa energy
destinations.vibe: Scottsdale is for polished resort days, rooftop drinks, and curated desert indulgence. From a Black traveler's point of view, it can feel less rooted in local culture than nearby Phoenix, but it works well for birthdays, girls' trips, golf weekends, and upscale relaxation.
destinations.safetyNotes: Scottsdale is generally one of the smoother visitor experiences in Arizona, especially around Old Town and the resort corridors. The main watch-outs are nightlife overreach, expensive tourist zones, and staying hydrated in desert heat while moving between venues.

destinations.tagline: Southern New Mexico sunshine with deep local history
destinations.vibe: Las Cruces is a practical, sunny, slower-paced city that works well if you want desert landscapes without Santa Fe prices. For Black travelers, the city's appeal comes from Southern New Mexico history, local pride, and access to the broader region.
destinations.safetyNotes: Las Cruces is usually straightforward for visitors, especially around major attractions and university-adjacent areas. It is more of a driving city than a walking city, and evenings are quieter than in larger Southwest destinations.

destinations.tagline: High-altitude culture, nightlife, and a city with real range
destinations.vibe: Bogotá feels big, intellectual, and layered, a city where museums, food, and nightlife can all sit in the same weekend. For Black travelers, it is strongest when you build an intentional neighborhood-based itinerary and treat it like a culture city first instead of expecting tropical Colombia vibes.
destinations.safetyNotes: Bogotá is very possible to enjoy, but it rewards city discipline. Stay in well-known districts, use rideshare at night, be careful with phones in crowded areas, and avoid flashing valuables because petty theft and opportunistic crime are still the main practical concern for visitors.

neighborhoods.name: Chapinero

destinations.tagline: Black Wall Street legacy with modern creative energy
destinations.vibe: Durham feels confident, contemporary and historically grounded, especially if you care about Black entrepreneurship and culture. For Black travelers, it is one of the strongest cities in this batch because the legacy of Hayti and Parrish Street still shapes the city’s identity in a way you can actually feel.
destinations.safetyNotes: Downtown Durham, American Tobacco and the core around major restaurants are the easiest areas to enjoy. Durham has real urban energy, so normal city caution matters, but many Black travelers find it affirming because Black history is not tucked away here; it is part of the city’s brand and public memory.

destinations.tagline: The most African city outside Africa — Bahia is the motherland reborn
destinations.vibe: The most African city outside of Africa — Candomble, capoeira, acaraje, and Yoruba traditions kept alive for 500 years.
destinations.safetyNotes: Salvador has one of Brazil's highest rates of Afro-Brazilian residents and an extraordinary cultural legacy. Pelourinho (the historic center) is safe during the day and packed with tourists. Be cautious with valuables and avoid displaying phones on the street. At night, stick to well-lit, crowded areas and use Uber. Salvador can be rough around the edges — travel smart, stay in tourist areas, and you'll have an extraordinary experience connecting with Africa's legacy in the Americas.

neighborhoods.name: Pelourinho
neighborhoods.description: UNESCO Historic Center — colonial baroque architecture, candomble terreiros, capoeira academies, and African-Brazilian restaurants.

neighborhoods.name: Barra
neighborhoods.description: Beachfront neighborhood with the iconic lighthouse, seafood restaurants, and a chill beach atmosphere.

neighborhoods.name: Rio Vermelho
neighborhoods.description: Salvador's bohemian neighborhood — bars, restaurants, the Iemanja festival beach, and local cultural life.

neighborhoods.name: Itapoa
neighborhoods.description: Beautiful beach neighborhood with calm waters, coconut vendors, and the real Salvador beach culture.

mustVisit.name: Candomble Ceremony
mustVisit.description: The most profound cultural experience in Brazil — observe (respectfully) an Afro-Brazilian religious ceremony honoring Yoruba orixas. Book through a cultural guide.

mustVisit.name: Capoeira at Pelourinho
mustVisit.description: Watch — and try — capoeira at the Fundacao Mestre Bimba or Grupo de Capoeira Angola Pelourinho. The art form born from enslaved Africans.

mustVisit.name: Mercado Modelo
mustVisit.description: Bahia's largest artisan market — formerly a slave holding facility, now a place of cultural healing. Lace, woodwork, cachaca, and music.

mustVisit.name: Acaraje on the Streets
mustVisit.description: The Bahian women in white (Baianas) selling acaraje (black-eyed pea fritters with vatapa and shrimp) on every corner. Sacred food — divine taste.

destinations.bestTimeToVisit: September-March (avoid heavy rains; Carnival in February is extraordinary)
destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens (up to 90 days)
destinations.ratingNotes: Most African city outside Africa; Candomblé; Afro-Brazilian culture central

destinations.safetyNotes: Seattle is generally safe and progressive. Capitol Hill, Central District, and the Central Business District are accessible for visitors. Seattle's well-known homeless issues in some areas require awareness.

destinations.tagline: Sedona has emerged as a top spiritual retreat and wellness destination among Black travelers. The...
destinations.vibe: Sedona has emerged as a top spiritual retreat and wellness destination among Black travelers. The red rock vortexes, luxury spas, and New Age culture attract Black women especially — popular with Black girl trip groups.
destinations.safetyNotes: Sedona is extremely safe and very welcoming. One of the more diverse tourist areas in Arizona. Generally progressive community with a welcoming vibe.

destinations.vibe: The Edmund Pettus Bridge where Bloody Sunday occurred is one of the most sacred sites in American history. Annual Bridge Crossing Jubilee brings thousands of pilgrims each March.
destinations.safetyNotes: Selma is a small, economically challenged city. The historic district and bridge area are safe for visitors. Plan your visit around the Jubilee if possible. Minimal tourist infrastructure otherwise.

destinations.tagline: Most visited national park in the US. Growing Black outdoors movement is discovering the Smokies....
destinations.vibe: Most visited national park in the US. Growing Black outdoors movement is discovering the Smokies. Gatlinburg and Pigeon Forge as entertainment hubs combined with stunning mountain scenery draw Black families year-round.
destinations.safetyNotes: The Smokies are welcoming for all visitors. Gatlinburg and Pigeon Forge are small tourist mountain towns — generally safe. Growing number of Black families and groups visit each year.

neighborhoods.name: Gatlinburg

neighborhoods.name: Pigeon Forge

destinations.tagline: South Africa's post-apartheid story resonates deeply with Black Americans. Cape Town's beauty, Ro...
destinations.vibe: South Africa's post-apartheid story resonates deeply with Black Americans. Cape Town's beauty, Robben Island, and the vibrant Black creative class in Johannesburg's Soweto make South Africa a top Black travel destination.
destinations.safetyNotes: South Africa has elevated crime rates — do not leave valuables visible in cars, avoid walking alone at night, and use Uber over street taxis. Cape Town's V&A Waterfront and Bo-Kaap are safe. Johannesburg's Maboneng and Sandton are safe. Crime is real but millions visit safely with awareness.

neighborhoods.name: V&A Waterfront (Cape Town)

destinations.tagline: America's oldest city with deep Black history — free Black communities existed here under Spanish...
destinations.vibe: America's oldest city with deep Black history — free Black communities existed here under Spanish rule. Civil rights sit-ins at Woolworth's in 1964 were pivotal. The Lincolnville neighborhood is a National Historic Landmark District.
destinations.safetyNotes: St. Augustine is a very safe, walkable tourist city. The historic district, Lincolnville, and the Matanzas waterfront are all accessible and welcoming.

neighborhoods.name: Lincolnville
neighborhoods.description: National Historic Landmark District — free Black community under Spanish, Jim Crow civil rights protests

mustVisit.name: Lincolnville Museum and Cultural Center

mustVisit.name: St. Augustine Slave Market Monument

mustVisit.name: St. Augustine Beach

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for US citizens

destinations.vibe: St. Croix is the most culturally rich of the US Virgin Islands with deep Afro-Caribbean heritage. Crucian culture is distinct — Crucian Christmas Festival is one of the Caribbean's best. No passport needed for Americans.
destinations.safetyNotes: St. Croix is very safe for tourists. Christiansted and Frederiksted are the two main towns — both accessible and welcoming. Buck Island Reef is a National Monument.

destinations.tagline: St. Louis's North Side has a rich Black history — Scott Joplin lived here, Chuck Berry is buried ...
destinations.vibe: St. Louis's North Side has a rich Black history — Scott Joplin lived here, Chuck Berry is buried here, and the Gateway Arch commemorates westward expansion that excluded Black people. A deeply important and often overlooked Black travel destination.
destinations.safetyNotes: St. Louis has elevated crime rates in some neighborhoods. The downtown area, Grand Center arts district, and Forest Park are safe for tourists. The Gateway Arch area is very safe. Research specific areas before venturing out.

destinations.tagline: Lowcountry calm with Gullah Geechee soul
destinations.vibe: Beaufort is quiet, scenic and deeply tied to Gullah Geechee culture, which gives it more spiritual and historical weight than a typical coastal town. For Black travelers, it feels less like a party destination and more like a place to exhale, learn and connect with lineage.
destinations.safetyNotes: The historic district, waterfront and St. Helena Island cultural sites are generally easy to navigate by day. The main consideration is that the area is spread out and slower-paced, so planning ahead matters; for Black travelers, the experience is often welcoming when centered on heritage spaces rather than generic resort expectations.

destinations.tagline: Vertical city drama, dim sum culture, and harbor views that always hit.
destinations.vibe: Hong Kong feels sharp, cinematic, and intensely urban, where efficient transit and neighborhood contrast make the city easy to sample fast. For Black travelers, it is usually more about curiosity and visible-foreigner attention than overt hostility, though nightlife and luxury retail settings can feel status-heavy.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally very safe for visitors by big-city standards, with strong transit and low everyday crime in tourist areas. Stay aware of your belongings in crowds, use standard nightlife caution, and keep an eye on any evolving local protest or political restrictions.

destinations.tagline: Spice-island warmth with beaches, hills, and a grounded soul

destinations.safetyNotes: Generally comfortable for visitors around Grand Anse and St. George’s, though hill driving and isolated stretches deserve attention. Use basic beach and nightlife caution and avoid leaving bags visible in parked cars.

destinations.tagline: Mediterranean ease with city polish and beach access.
destinations.vibe: Valencia feels lighter than Madrid and less hectic than Barcelona, which makes it easy to settle into. For Black travelers, it offers a relaxed coastal-city experience, though like many European beach cities, some spaces can feel more homogenous than the headlines suggest.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally comfortable, with petty theft and tourist-area scams more common than violent incidents. Use extra caution around nightlife strips, packed beaches, and transit stations.

destinations.tagline: Volcano views and colonial beauty with storybook streets
destinations.vibe: Antigua is one of those places that is as beautiful as people say, but it is still best enjoyed when you slow down and let the city breathe. For Black travelers, it works as a visual, café, and boutique-hotel destination with enough culture and day trips to keep the experience rich without feeling rushed.
destinations.safetyNotes: Antigua is generally easier than Guatemala City for most visitors, especially in the central grid and around major hotels and restaurants. Street robbery and pickpocketing can still happen, so avoid isolated walking routes after dark and use taxis or organized transport for trailheads and viewpoints.

destinations.tagline: Resilient, cultural, and richer than its headlines
destinations.vibe: Flint carries heavy history, but it also has creativity, civic pride, and a strong sense of survival. For Black travelers, it feels more like a place to understand community resilience than a polished vacation city.
destinations.safetyNotes: Flint is best approached with the same street smarts you would use in any struggling mid-sized city. Focus on curated stops downtown, cultural corridors, and daytime visits, and avoid assuming every area is set up for casual tourism.

destinations.tagline: Temple-lined calm, northern food, and a softer way into Thailand.
destinations.vibe: Chiang Mai feels slower, greener, and more breathable than Bangkok, with enough cafés, markets, and culture to stay interesting without exhausting you. For Black travelers, it often feels easier to settle into, especially if you want food, wellness, and low-drama day-to-day exploring.
destinations.safetyNotes: Generally one of the easier cities in the region for visitors to enjoy, though scooter accidents, petty theft, and tourist pricing still pop up. Stay aware in nightlife pockets and during smoky season if air quality matters to your trip.

mustVisit.name: Chiang Mai Night Bazaar

mustVisit.name: Doi Suthep

destinations.visaInfo: No visa required for many U.S. citizen tourist stays, subject to current Thailand entry rules; verify before travel.
destinations.ratingNotes: Peaceful temple city; colorism; limited BOP

destinations.city: St. Thomas / St. Croix / St. John
destinations.cityLower: st. thomas / st. croix / st. john
destinations.tagline: US territory — no passport needed. The Virgin Islands offer stunning Caribbean beaches, Danish co...
destinations.vibe: US territory — no passport needed. The Virgin Islands offer stunning Caribbean beaches, Danish colonial history, and a significant African diaspora heritage. St. Croix's Frederiksted has the best cultural immersion.
destinations.safetyNotes: St. Thomas and St. John are very safe for tourists. St. Croix has some areas requiring standard urban awareness. All three islands have warm, welcoming communities.

neighborhoods.name: Charlotte Amalie (St. Thomas)
neighborhoods.description: Capital city — major cruise port, duty-free shopping, historic Danish architecture

neighborhoods.name: Cruz Bay (St. John)
neighborhoods.description: Two-thirds of St. John is National Park — pristine beaches and snorkeling

neighborhoods.name: Frederiksted (St. Croix)
neighborhoods.description: St. Croix's most authentic town — Emancipation Park, Buck Island, local culture

mustVisit.name: Trunk Bay (St. John)

mustVisit.address: St. Thomas / St. Croix / St. John, US Virgin Islands

mustVisit.name: Emancipation Park (Frederiksted, St. Croix)

mustVisit.address: St. Thomas / St. Croix / St. John, US Virgin Islands

mustVisit.name: Buck Island Reef (St. Croix)
mustVisit.description: Only underwater National Monument in the US — world-class snorkeling

mustVisit.name: Magens Bay (St. Thomas)
